Impersonate Guide[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.3/impersonate.html#impersonate-guide "Link to this heading")

============================================================================================================================

You probably come across `curl_cffi` for it’s ability to impersonate browsers. Here is a tutorial on how to better impersonate using `curl_cffi`.

What is TLS and http/2, http/3 fingerprinting?[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.3/impersonate.html#what-is-tls-and-http-2-http-3-fingerprinting "Link to this heading")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### TLS and http/2[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.3/impersonate.html#tls-and-http-2 "Link to this heading")

TLS is the `s` in `https`. `https` has been uniformly deployed across the world. There are many extension and cipher suites a implementation can choose to use. According to the RFC, there are many valid combinations. But in reality, browser vendors tend to use fixed combinations, and these combinations can be used identify if the request is from a certain browser or an automated script. The digest of this combination is called a TLS fingerprints. The most common digesting method is called JA3.

Similar to TLS, there are a few settings in http/2 connection can be used to identify the source of a request. The most commonly used digesting method is proposed by Akamai, and called the Akamai http2 fingerprint.

To learn the details of TLS and http2 fingerprinting, you can read these great articles from lwthiker:

1.  [https://lwthiker.com/networks/2022/06/17/tls-fingerprinting.html](https://lwthiker.com/networks/2022/06/17/tls-fingerprinting.html)
    
2.  [https://lwthiker.com/networks/2022/06/17/http2-fingerprinting.html](https://lwthiker.com/networks/2022/06/17/http2-fingerprinting.html)
    

The format of JA3 and Akamai digest is briefly discussed below.

### http/3[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.3/impersonate.html#http-3 "Link to this heading")

As of http/3, the newest version of http. Basically, it’s http/2 reimplemented over QUIC, thus it can be fingerprinted in a similar way with http/2.

Http3 fingerprints has not yet been publicly exploited and reported. But given the rapidly increasing marketshare of http/3(35% of internet traffic), it is expected that some strict WAF vendors have begun to utilize http/3 fingerprinting.

It has also been noticed by many users, that, for a lot of sites, there is less or even none detection when using http/3.

`curl_cffi` provides TLS and http/2 impersonation in the open source version.

For http/3 impersonation and http/3 proxy support, please head over to [impersonate.pro](https://impersonate.pro/)
 for the commercial version of `curl_cffi`.

Supported browser impersonate targets[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.3/impersonate.html#supported-browser-impersonate-targets "Link to this heading")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

`curl_cffi` supports the same browser versions as supported by our [fork of curl-impersonate](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl-impersonate)
.

Browser versions will be added **only** when their fingerprints change. If you see a version, e.g. `chrome122`, was skipped, you can simply impersonate it with your own headers and the previous version.

If you are too busy to look up those details, you can try our commercial version at [impersonate.pro](https://impersonate.pro/)
, which has a weekly updated list of browser profiles and even more browser types.

If you are trying to impersonate a target other than a browser, use `ja3=...`, `akamai=...` and `extra_fp=...` to specify your own customized fingerprints. See below for details.

*   chrome99
    
*   chrome100
    
*   chrome101
    
*   chrome104
    
*   chrome107
    
*   chrome110
    
*   chrome116 1
    
*   chrome119 1
    
*   chrome120 1
    
*   chrome123 3
    
*   chrome124 3
    
*   chrome131 4
    
*   chrome133a 5 6
    
*   chrome136 7
    
*   chrome99\_android
    
*   chrome131\_android 4
    
*   edge99
    
*   edge101
    
*   safari153 2
    
*   safari155 2
    
*   safari170 1
    
*   safari172\_ios 1
    
*   safari180 4
    
*   safari180\_ios 4
    
*   safari184 7
    
*   safari184\_ios 7
    
*   firefox133 5
    
*   tor145 7
    

Notes:

1.  Added in version `0.6.0`.
    
2.  Fixed in version `0.6.0`, previous http2 fingerprints were [not correct](https://github.com/lwthiker/curl-impersonate/issues/215)
    .
    
3.  Added in version `0.7.0`.
    
4.  Added in version `0.8.0`.
    
5.  Added in version `0.9.0`.
    
6.  The version postfix `-a``(e.g. ``chrome133a`) means that this is an alternative version, i.e. the fingerprint has not been officially updated by browser, but has been observed because of A/B testing.
    
7.  Added in version `0.11.0`
    

Which target version to use?[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.3/impersonate.html#which-target-version-to-use "Link to this heading")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Generally speaking, you should use the latest Chrome or Safari versions. As of v0.11, they’re `chrome136`, `safari184` and `safari184_ios`. To always impersonate the latest available browser versions, you can simply use `chrome`, `firefox`, `safari` and `chrome_android`, `safari_ios`.

import curl\_cffi

curl\_cffi.get(url, impersonate\="chrome")

Tips:

iOS has restrictions on WebView and TLS libs, so `safari_*_ios` should work for a lot of apps. If you encountered an android app with custom fingerprints, you can try the `safari_ios` fingerprints, given that this app should have an iOS version.

How to use my own fingerprints? e.g. okhttp[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.3/impersonate.html#how-to-use-my-own-fingerprints-e-g-okhttp "Link to this heading")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Use `ja3=...`, `akamai=...` and `extra_fp=...`.

You can retrieve the JA3 and Akamai strings using tools like WireShark or from TLS fingerprinting sites.

\# OKHTTP impersonatation examples
\# credits: https://github.com/bogdanfinn/tls-client/blob/master/profiles/contributed\_custom\_profiles.go

url \= "https://tls.browserleaks.com/json"

okhttp4\_android10\_ja3 \= ",".join(
    \[\
        "771",\
        "4865-4866-4867-49195-49196-52393-49199-49200-52392-49171-49172-156-157-47-53",\
        "0-23-65281-10-11-35-16-5-13-51-45-43-21",\
        "29-23-24",\
        "0",\
    \]
)

okhttp4\_android10\_akamai \= "4:16777216|16711681|0|m,p,a,s"

extra\_fp \= {
    "tls\_signature\_algorithms": \[\
        "ecdsa\_secp256r1\_sha256",\
        "rsa\_pss\_rsae\_sha256",\
        "rsa\_pkcs1\_sha256",\
        "ecdsa\_secp384r1\_sha384",\
        "rsa\_pss\_rsae\_sha384",\
        "rsa\_pkcs1\_sha384",\
        "rsa\_pss\_rsae\_sha512",\
        "rsa\_pkcs1\_sha512",\
        "rsa\_pkcs1\_sha1",\
    \]
    \# other options:
    \# tls\_min\_version: int = CurlSslVersion.TLSv1\_2
    \# tls\_grease: bool = False
    \# tls\_permute\_extensions: bool = False
    \# tls\_cert\_compression: Literal\["zlib", "brotli"\] = "brotli"
    \# tls\_signature\_algorithms: Optional\[List\[str\]\] = None
    \# http2\_stream\_weight: int = 256
    \# http2\_stream\_exclusive: int = 1

    \# See requests/impersonate.py and tests/unittest/test\_impersonate.py for more examples
}

r \= curl\_cffi.get(
    url, ja3\=okhttp4\_android10\_ja3, akamai\=okhttp4\_android10\_akamai, extra\_fp\=extra\_fp
)
print(r.json())

### JA3 and Akamai String Format[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.3/impersonate.html#ja3-and-akamai-string-format "Link to this heading")

A JA3 string is a simple, comma-separated representation of the key fields in a TLS ClientHello. It consists of five parts:

*   SSL/TLS Version, The numeric version the client requests (e.g. 771 for TLS 1.2).
    
*   Cipher Suites, A hyphen-separated list of all cipher suite IDs offered by the client (e.g. 4865-4866-4867-49195-49196).
    
*   Extension IDs, A hyphen-separated list of all TLS extension numbers the client includes (e.g. 0-11-10-35-16-5).
    
*   Supported Groups (aka “Elliptic Curves”), A hyphen-separated list of curve IDs the client supports for ECDHE (e.g. 29-23-24).
    
*   EC Point Formats, A hyphen-separated list of the point‐format IDs (almost always just 0 for “uncompressed”) (e.g. 0).
    

They’re concatenated in that exact order, with commas between fields. For example:

771,4865\-4866\-4867\-49195\-49196,0\-11\-10\-35\-16\-5,29\-23\-24,0

Note that Chrome permutes the extension order on each request, so there is a new format called JA3N, which uses sorted extension\_id list.

The Akamai HTTP/2 fingerprint string encodes four client‐controlled protocol parameters, joined by the pipe character (|):

*   SETTINGS, A semicolon‐separated list of ID:value pairs from the client’s initial SETTINGS frame. Each ID is a standard HTTP/2 setting identifier (e.g. 1 for HEADER\_TABLE\_SIZE, 4 for INITIAL\_WINDOW\_SIZE), and value is the client’s chosen value for that setting
    
*   WINDOW\_UPDATE, A single integer: the value the client sends in its first WINDOW\_UPDATE frame (or 0 if none was sent)
    
*   PRIORITY, Zero or more priority‐frame tuples, each formatted as `StreamID:ExclusiveBit:DependentStreamID:Weight`. Multiple tuples are comma-separated. This captures any PRIORITY frames the client issues before sending headers
    
*   Pseudo-Header Order, The sequence in which the client sends HTTP/2 pseudo-headers in its request HEADERS frame, encoded as comma-separated single-letter codes:
    

Putting it all together, an example fingerprint might look like:

1:65536;4:131072;5:16384|12517377|3:0:0:201|m,p,a,s

where:

SETTINGS \= 1:65536;4:131072;5:16384
WINDOW\_UPDATE \= 12517377
PRIORITY \= 3:0:0:201
Pseudo\-Header Order \= m,p,a,s

Although JA3 and Akamai fingerprint string already captures many of the aspects of a Hello Packet, there are still some fields are not covered and can be used to detect you. This is when the `extra_fp` option comes in, each field of this dict is pretty easy to understand. You should first set the ja3 and akamai string, then check if you have the identical fingerprint like your target. If not, use the `extra_fp` to further refine your impersonation.

### Using CURLOPTs[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.3/impersonate.html#using-curlopts "Link to this heading")

The other way is to use the `curlopt` s to specify exactly which options you want to change.

To modify them, use `curl.setopt(CurlOpt, value)`, for example:

import curl\_cffi
from curl\_cffi import Curl, CurlOpt

c \= Curl()
c.setopt(CurlOpt.HTTP2\_PSEUDO\_HEADERS\_ORDER, "masp")

\# or
curl\_cffi.get(url, curl\_options\={CurlOpt.HTTP2\_PSEUDO\_HEADERS\_ORDER, "masp"})

Here are a list of options:

For TLS/JA3 fingerprints:

*   [https://curl.se/libcurl/c/CURLOPT\_SSL\_CIPHER\_LIST.html](https://curl.se/libcurl/c/CURLOPT_SSL_CIPHER_LIST.html)
    

and non-standard TLS options created for this project:

*   `CURLOPT_SSL_ENABLE_ALPS`
    
*   `CURLOPT_SSL_SIG_HASH_ALGS`
    
*   `CURLOPT_SSL_CERT_COMPRESSION`
    
*   `CURLOPT_SSL_ENABLE_TICKET`
    
*   `CURLOPT_SSL_PERMUTE_EXTENSIONS`
    

For Akamai http2 fingerprints, you can fully customize the 3 parts:

*   `CURLOPT_HTTP2_PSEUDO_HEADERS_ORDER`, sets http2 pseudo header order, for example: `masp` (non-standard HTTP/2 options created for this project).
    
*   `CURLOPT_HTTP2_SETTINGS` sets the settings frame values, for example `1:65536;3:1000;4:6291456;6:262144` (non-standard HTTP/2 options created for this project).
    
*   `CURLOPT_HTTP2_WINDOW_UPDATE` sets initial window update value for http2, for example `15663105` (non-standard HTTP/2 options created for this project).
    

For a complete list of options and explanation, see the [\`curl-impersoante README\`\_](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.3/impersonate.html#id2)
.

### How to toggle firefox-specific extensions?[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.3/impersonate.html#how-to-toggle-firefox-specific-extensions "Link to this heading")

There are a few special extensions from firefox that you should add extra options by `extra_fp`:

Extension 34: delegated credentials

extra\_fp \= {
    "tls\_delegated\_credential": "ecdsa\_secp256r1\_sha256:ecdsa\_secp384r1\_sha384:ecdsa\_secp521r1\_sha512:ecdsa\_sha1"
}

\# Note that the ja3 string also includes extensiion: 34
ja3 \= "771,4865-4867-4866-49195-49199-52393-52392-49196-49200-49162-49161-49171-49172-156-157-47-53,0-23-65281-10-11-35-16-5-34-18-51-43-13-45-28-27-65037,4588-29-23-24-25-256-257,0"

r \= curl\_cffi.get(url, ja3\=ja3, extra\_fp\=extra\_fp)

Extension 28: record size limit

extra\_fp \= {
    "tls\_record\_size\_limit": 4001
}

\# Note that the ja3 string also includes extensiion: 28
ja3 \= "771,4865-4867-4866-49195-49199-52393-52392-49196-49200-49162-49161-49171-49172-156-157-47-53,0-23-65281-10-11-35-16-5-34-18-51-43-13-45-28-27-65037,4588-29-23-24-25-256-257,0"

r \= curl\_cffi.get(url, ja3\=ja3, extra\_fp\=extra\_fp)

How to check if my impersonation is working?[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.3/impersonate.html#how-to-check-if-my-impersonation-is-working "Link to this heading")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The most reliable way is to use WireShark, and compare packets from `curl_cffi` and your targets.

If it’s challenging for you to use WireShark, you can use the following sites for JA3 and Akamai fingerprints:

1.  [https://tls.browserleaks.com/json](https://tls.browserleaks.com/json)
    
2.  [https://tls.peet.ws/api/all](https://tls.peet.ws/api/all)
    
3.  [https://scrapfly.io/web-scraping-tools/browser-fingerprint](https://scrapfly.io/web-scraping-tools/browser-fingerprint)
    

For http/3 fingerprints, use our service:

1.  [https://fp.impersonate.pro/api/http3](https://fp.impersonate.pro/api/http3)
    

I’m still being detected even if I impersonated correctly[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.3/impersonate.html#i-m-still-being-detected-even-if-i-impersonated-correctly "Link to this heading")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

First, JA3 and akamai fingerprints are not comprehensive, there are other fields that can be detected, we have a few more options listed in `extra_fp`. Be sure to also check them.

Note

Since `curl-impersonate` was posted on [Hacker News](https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=42547820)
, some features and behaviors of `curl_cffi` is being detected by professional players. If we continue to fix these niche behavior in public, it would soon be noticed by those providers.

In short, if you are using curl\_cffi in production and you are sure about being blocked by TLS or http detection, try the pro version.

Should I randomize my fingerprints for each request?[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.3/impersonate.html#should-i-randomize-my-fingerprints-for-each-request "Link to this heading")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You can choose a random version from the list above, like:

random.choice(\["chrome119", "chrome120", ...\])

However, be aware of the browser market share, very old versions are not good choices.

Generally, you should not try to generate a customized random fingerprints. The reason is that, for a given browser version, the fingerprints are fixed. If you create a new random fingerprints, the server is easy to know that you are not using a typical browser.

If you were thinking about `ja3`, and not `ja3n`, then the fingerprints is already randomized, due to the `extension permutation` feature introduced in Chrome 110.

As far as we know, most websites use an allowlist, not a blocklist to filter out bot traffic. So do not expect random ja3 fingerprints would work in the wild.

Moreover, do not generate random ja3 strings. There are certain limits for a valid ja3 string. For example:

*   TLS 1.3 ciphers must be at the front.
    
*   GREASE extension must be the first.
    
*   etc.
    

You should copy ja3 strings from sniffing tools, not generate them, unless you can make sure all the requirements are met.

Can I change JavaScript fingerprints with this library?[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.3/impersonate.html#can-i-change-javascript-fingerprints-with-this-library "Link to this heading")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

No, you can not. As the name suggests, JavaScript fingerprints are generated using JavaScript APIs provided by real browsers. `curl_cffi` is a python binding to a C library, with no browser or JavaScript runtime under the hood.

If you need to impersonate browsers on the JavaScript perspective, you can search for “Anti-detect Browser”, “Playwright stealth” and similar keywords. Or simply use a commercial plan from our sponsors.

# Cookies — curl_cffi  documentation

Cookies[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.2/cookies.html#cookies "Link to this heading")

===================================================================================================

How to save and load cookies[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.2/cookies.html#how-to-save-and-load-cookies "Link to this heading")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Do not use `get_dict` to dump and load cookies. Cookies are more than just plain key-value pairs.

Using pickle:

\# example from: https://github.com/encode/httpx/issues/895
import pickle
\# import httpx
from curl\_cffi import requests

def save\_cookies(client):
    with open("cookies.pk", "wb") as f:
        pickle.dump(client.cookies.jar.\_cookies, f)

def load\_cookies():
    if not os.path.isfile("cookies.pk"):
        return None
    with open("cookies.pk", "rb") as f:
        return pickle.load(f)

\# client = httpx.Client(cookies=load\_cookies())
client \= requests.Session()
client.get("https://httpbin.org/cookies/set/foo/bar")
save\_cookies(client)

client \= requests.Session()
client.cookies.jar.\_cookies.update(load\_cookies())
print(client.cookies.get("foo"))

Using mozilla cookie jar:

See: [https://github.com/lexiforest/curl\_cffi/issues/381](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl_cffi/issues/381)

TODO: expose libcurl’s native cookies.txt support.

[curl\_cffi](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.2/index.html)

====================================================================

# Advanced Usage — curl_cffi  documentation

Advanced Usage[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.2/advanced.html#advanced-usage "Link to this heading")

==================================================================================================================

low-level curl API[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.2/advanced.html#low-level-curl-api "Link to this heading")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Alternatively, you can use the low-level curl-like API:

from curl\_cffi import Curl, CurlOpt
from io import BytesIO

buffer \= BytesIO()
c \= Curl()
c.setopt(CurlOpt.URL, b'https://tls.browserleaks.com/json')
c.setopt(CurlOpt.WRITEDATA, buffer)

c.impersonate("chrome124")

c.perform()
c.close()
body \= buffer.getvalue()
print(body.decode())

scrapy integrations[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.2/advanced.html#scrapy-integrations "Link to this heading")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

If you are using scrapy, check out these middlewares:

*   \[tieyongjie/scrapy-fingerprint\]([https://github.com/tieyongjie/scrapy-fingerprint](https://github.com/tieyongjie/scrapy-fingerprint)
    )
    
*   \[jxlil/scrapy-impersonate\]([https://github.com/jxlil/scrapy-impersonate](https://github.com/jxlil/scrapy-impersonate)
    )
    

Using with eventlet/gevent[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.2/advanced.html#using-with-eventlet-gevent "Link to this heading")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Just set `thread` to eventlet or gevent.

from curl\_cffi import requests

s \= requests.Session(thread\="eventlet")
s.get(url)

[curl\_cffi](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.2/index.html)

====================================================================

low-level curl API[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.0/advanced.html#low-level-curl-api "Link to this heading")

Alternatively, you can use the low-level curl-like API:

from curl\_cffi import Curl, CurlOpt

buffer \= BytesIO()

c.perform()

# Advanced Usage — curl_cffi  documentation

Advanced Usage[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.1/advanced.html#advanced-usage "Link to this heading")

===================================================================================================================

Low-level curl API[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.1/advanced.html#low-level-curl-api "Link to this heading")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Alternatively, you can use the low-level curl-like API:

from curl\_cffi import Curl, CurlOpt
from io import BytesIO

buffer \= BytesIO()
c \= Curl()
c.setopt(CurlOpt.URL, b'https://tls.browserleaks.com/json')
c.setopt(CurlOpt.WRITEDATA, buffer)

c.impersonate("chrome124")

c.perform()
c.close()
body \= buffer.getvalue()
print(body.decode())

Scrapy integrations[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.1/advanced.html#scrapy-integrations "Link to this heading")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

If you are using scrapy, check out these middlewares:

*   \[tieyongjie/scrapy-fingerprint\]([https://github.com/tieyongjie/scrapy-fingerprint](https://github.com/tieyongjie/scrapy-fingerprint)
    )
    
*   \[jxlil/scrapy-impersonate\]([https://github.com/jxlil/scrapy-impersonate](https://github.com/jxlil/scrapy-impersonate)
    )
    

Using with eventlet/gevent[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.1/advanced.html#using-with-eventlet-gevent "Link to this heading")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Just set `thread` to eventlet or gevent.

from curl\_cffi import requests

s \= requests.Session(thread\="eventlet")
s.get(url)

As a urllib3/requests adapter[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.1/advanced.html#as-a-urllib3-requests-adapter "Link to this heading")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You can also use curl-cffi as a requests adapter via [curl-adapter](https://github.com/el1s7/curl-adapter)
. In this way, you get the full functionality of requests.

import requests
from curl\_adapter import CurlCffiAdapter

session \= requests.Session()
session.mount("http://", CurlCffiAdapter())
session.mount("https://", CurlCffiAdapter())

\# just use requests session like you normally would
session.get("https://example.com")

As a httpx transport[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.1/advanced.html#as-a-httpx-transport "Link to this heading")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You can also use curl-cffi as a httpx transport via [httpx-curl-cffi](https://github.com/vgavro/httpx-curl-cffi)
. With this, you get the full functionality of httpx.

from httpx import Client, AsyncClient
from httpx\_curl\_cffi import CurlTransport, AsyncCurlTransport, CurlOpt

client \= Client(transport\=CurlTransport(impersonate\="chrome", default\_headers\=True))
client.get("https://tools.scrapfly.io/api/fp/ja3")

async\_client \= AsyncClient(transport\=AsyncCurlTransport(
    impersonate\="chrome",
    default\_headers\=True,
    \# required for parallel requests, see curl\_cffi issues below
    curl\_options\={CurlOpt.FRESH\_CONNECT: True}
))

Keeping session alive in http/2[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.1/advanced.html#keeping-session-alive-in-http-2 "Link to this heading")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

With http/2, you can optionally send a ping frame to keep the connection alive when not actively using it.

import curl\_cffi

s \= Session()
s.get("https://example.com")
s.upkeep()

[curl\_cffi](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.1/index.html)

=====================================================================

# Compatibility with requests — curl_cffi  documentation

### Navigation

*   [index](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.4/genindex.html "General Index")
    
*   [next](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.4/cookies.html "Cookies")
     |
*   [previous](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.4/advanced.html "Advanced Topics")
     |
*   [curl\_cffi documentation](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.4/index.html)
     »
*   [Compatibility with requests](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.4/vs-requests.html)
    

Compatibility with requests[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.4/vs-requests.html#compatibility-with-requests "Link to this heading")

================================================================================================================================================

Although we try our best to mimic the requests API, some functionality is not easy to implement and left out. Here are a list of known incompatibilities:

*   files API are slightly different, but more error-proof.
    
*   retries are not supported yet, tracked in \[#24\]([https://github.com/lexiforest/curl\_cffi/issues/24](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl_cffi/issues/24)
    )
    
*   redirect history are not supported, tracked in \[#82\]([https://github.com/lexiforest/curl\_cffi/issues/82](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl_cffi/issues/82)
    )
    
*   empty-domains cookies may lost during redirects, tracked in \[#55\]([https://github.com/lexiforest/curl\_cffi/issues/55](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl_cffi/issues/55)
    )
    
*   response object can not be pickled.
    
*   The `requests` proxies dict is supported, but we prefer `proxy=...`, unless you really use different proxies for http and https.
    
*   You can use use transports/adapters, instead, you can use `curl_cffi` as adapter for `reuqests`.
    

Transports and Adapters[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.4/vs-requests.html#transports-and-adapters "Link to this heading")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

`curl_cffi` is deeply coupled with `libcurl-impersonate`. Unlike `requests` or `httpx`, There is no way to use a different networking library or mount different adapters.

Alternatively, you can use `curl-cffi` as a requests adapter via [curl-adapter](https://github.com/el1s7/curl-adapter)
. In this way, you get the full functionality of requests.

### [Table of Contents](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.4/index.html)

*   [Compatibility with requests](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.4/vs-requests.html#)
    *   [Transports and Adapters](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.4/vs-requests.html#transports-and-adapters)

     |

     »

     »
*   [Supported browser impersonate targets](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.12.0/impersonate/targets.html)
    

Supported browser impersonate targets[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.12.0/impersonate/targets.html#supported-browser-impersonate-targets "Link to this heading")

============================================================================================================================================================================

`curl_cffi` supports the same browser versions as supported by our [fork of curl-impersonate](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl-impersonate)
.

Browser versions will be added **only** when their fingerprints change. If you see a version, e.g. `chrome122`, was skipped, you can simply impersonate it with your own headers and the previous version.

If you are too busy to look up those details, you can try our commercial version at [impersonate.pro](https://impersonate.pro/)
, which has a weekly updated list of browser profiles and even more browser types.

If you are trying to impersonate a target other than a browser, use `ja3=...`, `akamai=...` and `extra_fp=...` to specify your own customized fingerprints. See below for details.

*   chrome99
    
*   chrome100
    
*   chrome101
    
*   chrome104
    
*   chrome107
    
*   chrome110
    
*   chrome116 1
    
*   chrome119 1
    
*   chrome120 1
    
*   chrome123 3
    
*   chrome124 3
    
*   chrome131 4
    
*   chrome133a 5 6
    
*   chrome136 7
    
*   chrome99\_android
    
*   chrome131\_android 4
    
*   edge99
    
*   edge101
    
*   safari153 2
    
*   safari155 2
    
*   safari170 1
    
*   safari172\_ios 1
    
*   safari180 4
    
*   safari180\_ios 4
    
*   safari184 7
    
*   safari184\_ios 7
    
*   safari260 8
    
*   safari260\_ios 8
    
*   firefox133 5
    
*   tor145 7
    

Notes:

1.  Added in version `0.6.0`.
    
2.  Fixed in version `0.6.0`, previous http2 fingerprints were [not correct](https://github.com/lwthiker/curl-impersonate/issues/215)
    .
    
3.  Added in version `0.7.0`.
    
4.  Added in version `0.8.0`.
    
5.  Added in version `0.9.0`.
    
6.  The version postfix `-a``(e.g. ``chrome133a`) means that this is an alternative version, i.e. the fingerprint has not been officially updated by browser, but has been observed because of A/B testing.
    
7.  Added in version `0.11.0`
    
8.  Added in version `0.12.0`
    

Which target version to use?[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.12.0/impersonate/targets.html#which-target-version-to-use "Link to this heading")

=========================================================================================================================================================

Generally speaking, you should use the latest Chrome or Safari versions. As of v0.11, they’re `chrome136`, `safari184` and `safari184_ios`. To always impersonate the latest available browser versions, you can simply use `chrome`, `firefox`, `safari` and `chrome_android`, `safari_ios`.

import curl\_cffi

curl\_cffi.get(url, impersonate\="chrome")

Tips:

iOS has restrictions on WebView and TLS libs, so `safari_*_ios` should work for a lot of apps. If you encountered an android app with custom fingerprints, you can try the `safari_ios` fingerprints, given that this app should have an iOS version.

*   [Which target version to use?](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.12.0/impersonate/targets.html#which-target-version-to-use)

*   files API are slightly different, but more error-proof.

# How to use my own fingerprints? e.g. okhttp — curl_cffi  documentation

### Navigation

*   [index](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.13.0/genindex.html "General Index")
    
*   [next](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.13.0/impersonate/psk.html "TLS PSK(41) Extension")
     |
*   [previous](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.13.0/impersonate/targets.html "Supported browser impersonate targets")
     |
*   [curl\_cffi documentation](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.13.0/index.html)
     »
*   [Impersonate Guide](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.13.0/impersonate/_index.html)
     »
*   [How to use my own fingerprints? e.g. okhttp](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.13.0/impersonate/customize.html)
    

How to use my own fingerprints? e.g. okhttp[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.13.0/impersonate/customize.html#how-to-use-my-own-fingerprints-e-g-okhttp "Link to this heading")

========================================================================================================================================================================================

Use `ja3=...`, `akamai=...` and `extra_fp=...`.

You can retrieve the JA3 and Akamai strings using tools like WireShark or from TLS fingerprinting sites.

\# OKHTTP impersonatation examples
\# credits: https://github.com/bogdanfinn/tls-client/blob/master/profiles/contributed\_custom\_profiles.go

url \= "https://tls.browserleaks.com/json"

okhttp4\_android10\_ja3 \= ",".join(
    \[\
        "771",\
        "4865-4866-4867-49195-49196-52393-49199-49200-52392-49171-49172-156-157-47-53",\
        "0-23-65281-10-11-35-16-5-13-51-45-43-21",\
        "29-23-24",\
        "0",\
    \]
)

okhttp4\_android10\_akamai \= "4:16777216|16711681|0|m,p,a,s"

extra\_fp \= {
    "tls\_signature\_algorithms": \[\
        "ecdsa\_secp256r1\_sha256",\
        "rsa\_pss\_rsae\_sha256",\
        "rsa\_pkcs1\_sha256",\
        "ecdsa\_secp384r1\_sha384",\
        "rsa\_pss\_rsae\_sha384",\
        "rsa\_pkcs1\_sha384",\
        "rsa\_pss\_rsae\_sha512",\
        "rsa\_pkcs1\_sha512",\
        "rsa\_pkcs1\_sha1",\
    \]
    \# other options:
    \# tls\_min\_version: int = CurlSslVersion.TLSv1\_2
    \# tls\_grease: bool = False
    \# tls\_permute\_extensions: bool = False
    \# tls\_cert\_compression: Literal\["zlib", "brotli"\] = "brotli"
    \# tls\_signature\_algorithms: Optional\[List\[str\]\] = None
    \# http2\_stream\_weight: int = 256
    \# http2\_stream\_exclusive: int = 1

    \# See requests/impersonate.py and tests/unittest/test\_impersonate.py for more examples
}

r \= curl\_cffi.get(
    url, ja3\=okhttp4\_android10\_ja3, akamai\=okhttp4\_android10\_akamai, extra\_fp\=extra\_fp
)
print(r.json())

JA3 and Akamai String Format[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.13.0/impersonate/customize.html#ja3-and-akamai-string-format "Link to this heading")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

A JA3 string is a simple, comma-separated representation of the key fields in a TLS ClientHello. It consists of five parts:

*   SSL/TLS Version, The numeric version the client requests (e.g. 771 for TLS 1.2).
    
*   Cipher Suites, A hyphen-separated list of all cipher suite IDs offered by the client (e.g. 4865-4866-4867-49195-49196).
    
*   Extension IDs, A hyphen-separated list of all TLS extension numbers the client includes (e.g. 0-11-10-35-16-5).
    
*   Supported Groups (aka “Elliptic Curves”), A hyphen-separated list of curve IDs the client supports for ECDHE (e.g. 29-23-24).
    
*   EC Point Formats, A hyphen-separated list of the point‐format IDs (almost always just 0 for “uncompressed”) (e.g. 0).
    

They’re concatenated in that exact order, with commas between fields. For example:

771,4865\-4866\-4867\-49195\-49196,0\-11\-10\-35\-16\-5,29\-23\-24,0

Note that Chrome permutes the extension order on each request, so there is a new format called JA3N, which uses sorted extension\_id list.

The Akamai HTTP/2 fingerprint string encodes four client‐controlled protocol parameters, joined by the pipe character (|):

*   SETTINGS, A semicolon‐separated list of ID:value pairs from the client’s initial SETTINGS frame. Each ID is a standard HTTP/2 setting identifier (e.g. 1 for HEADER\_TABLE\_SIZE, 4 for INITIAL\_WINDOW\_SIZE), and value is the client’s chosen value for that setting
    
*   WINDOW\_UPDATE, A single integer: the value the client sends in its first WINDOW\_UPDATE frame (or 0 if none was sent)
    
*   PRIORITY, Zero or more priority‐frame tuples, each formatted as `StreamID:ExclusiveBit:DependentStreamID:Weight`. Multiple tuples are comma-separated. This captures any PRIORITY frames the client issues before sending headers
    
*   Pseudo-Header Order, The sequence in which the client sends HTTP/2 pseudo-headers in its request HEADERS frame, encoded as comma-separated single-letter codes:
    

Putting it all together, an example fingerprint might look like:

1:65536;4:131072;5:16384|12517377|3:0:0:201|m,p,a,s

where:

SETTINGS \= 1:65536;4:131072;5:16384
WINDOW\_UPDATE \= 12517377
PRIORITY \= 3:0:0:201
Pseudo\-Header Order \= m,p,a,s

Although JA3 and Akamai fingerprint string already captures many of the aspects of a Hello Packet, there are still some fields are not covered and can be used to detect you. This is when the `extra_fp` option comes in, each field of this dict is pretty easy to understand. You should first set the ja3 and akamai string, then check if you have the identical fingerprint like your target. If not, use the `extra_fp` to further refine your impersonation.

Using CURLOPTs[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.13.0/impersonate/customize.html#using-curlopts "Link to this heading")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The other way is to use the `curlopt` s to specify exactly which options you want to change.

To modify them, use `curl.setopt(CurlOpt, value)`, for example:

import curl\_cffi
from curl\_cffi import Curl, CurlOpt

c \= Curl()
c.setopt(CurlOpt.HTTP2\_PSEUDO\_HEADERS\_ORDER, "masp")

\# or
curl\_cffi.get(url, curl\_options\={CurlOpt.HTTP2\_PSEUDO\_HEADERS\_ORDER, "masp"})

Here are a list of options:

For TLS/JA3 fingerprints:

*   [https://curl.se/libcurl/c/CURLOPT\_SSL\_CIPHER\_LIST.html](https://curl.se/libcurl/c/CURLOPT_SSL_CIPHER_LIST.html)
    

and non-standard TLS options created for this project:

*   `CURLOPT_SSL_ENABLE_ALPS`
    
*   `CURLOPT_SSL_SIG_HASH_ALGS`
    
*   `CURLOPT_SSL_CERT_COMPRESSION`
    
*   `CURLOPT_SSL_ENABLE_TICKET`
    
*   `CURLOPT_SSL_PERMUTE_EXTENSIONS`
    

For Akamai http2 fingerprints, you can fully customize the 3 parts:

*   `CURLOPT_HTTP2_PSEUDO_HEADERS_ORDER`, sets http2 pseudo header order, for example: `masp` (non-standard HTTP/2 options created for this project).
    
*   `CURLOPT_HTTP2_SETTINGS` sets the settings frame values, for example `1:65536;3:1000;4:6291456;6:262144` (non-standard HTTP/2 options created for this project).
    
*   `CURLOPT_HTTP2_WINDOW_UPDATE` sets initial window update value for http2, for example `15663105` (non-standard HTTP/2 options created for this project).
    

For a complete list of options and explanation, see the [\`curl-impersoante README\`\_](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.13.0/impersonate/customize.html#id1)
.

How to toggle firefox-specific extensions?[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.13.0/impersonate/customize.html#how-to-toggle-firefox-specific-extensions "Link to this heading")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

There are a few special extensions from firefox that you should add extra options by `extra_fp`:

Extension 34: delegated credentials

extra\_fp \= {
    "tls\_delegated\_credential": "ecdsa\_secp256r1\_sha256:ecdsa\_secp384r1\_sha384:ecdsa\_secp521r1\_sha512:ecdsa\_sha1"
}

\# Note that the ja3 string also includes extensiion: 34
ja3 \= "771,4865-4867-4866-49195-49199-52393-52392-49196-49200-49162-49161-49171-49172-156-157-47-53,0-23-65281-10-11-35-16-5-34-18-51-43-13-45-28-27-65037,4588-29-23-24-25-256-257,0"

r \= curl\_cffi.get(url, ja3\=ja3, extra\_fp\=extra\_fp)

Extension 28: record size limit

extra\_fp \= {
    "tls\_record\_size\_limit": 4001
}

\# Note that the ja3 string also includes extensiion: 28
ja3 \= "771,4865-4867-4866-49195-49199-52393-52392-49196-49200-49162-49161-49171-49172-156-157-47-53,0-23-65281-10-11-35-16-5-34-18-51-43-13-45-28-27-65037,4588-29-23-24-25-256-257,0"

r \= curl\_cffi.get(url, ja3\=ja3, extra\_fp\=extra\_fp)

### [Table of Contents](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.13.0/index.html)

# Compatibility with requests — curl_cffi  documentation

Compatibility with requests[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.2/vs-requests.html#compatibility-with-requests "Link to this heading")

================================================================================================================================================

Although we try our best to mimic the requests API, some functionality is not easy to implement and left out. Here are a list of known incompatibilities:

*   files API are slightly different, but more error-proof.
    
*   retries are not supported yet, tracked in \[#24\]([https://github.com/lexiforest/curl\_cffi/issues/24](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl_cffi/issues/24)
    )
    
*   redirect history are not supported, tracked in \[#82\]([https://github.com/lexiforest/curl\_cffi/issues/82](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl_cffi/issues/82)
    )
    
*   empty-domains cookies may lost during redirects, tracked in \[#55\]([https://github.com/lexiforest/curl\_cffi/issues/55](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl_cffi/issues/55)
    )
    
*   response object can not be pickled.
    

Alternatively, you can also use curl-cffi as a requests adapter via [curl-adapter](https://github.com/el1s7/curl-adapter)
. In this way, you get the full functionality of requests.

[curl\_cffi](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.2/index.html)

=====================================================================

Compatibility with requests[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.10.0/vs-requests.html#compatibility-with-requests "Link to this heading")

================================================================================================================================================

Although we try our best to mimic the requests API, some functionality is not easy to implement and left out. Here are a list of known incompatibilities:

*   files API are slightly different, but more error-proof.
    
*   retries are not supported yet, tracked in \[#24\]([https://github.com/lexiforest/curl\_cffi/issues/24](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl_cffi/issues/24)

    )
    

Alternatively, you can also use curl-cffi as a requests adapter via [curl-adapter](https://github.com/el1s7/curl-adapter)

# Community — curl_cffi  documentation

### Navigation

*   [index](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.3/genindex.html "General Index")

     |

     |
*   [curl\_cffi documentation](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.3/index.html)
     »
*   [Community](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.3/community.html)
    

Community[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.3/community.html#community "Link to this heading")

==========================================================================================================

Scrapy integrations[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.3/community.html#scrapy-integrations "Link to this heading")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

If you are using scrapy, check out these middlewares:

*   \[tieyongjie/scrapy-fingerprint\]([https://github.com/tieyongjie/scrapy-fingerprint](https://github.com/tieyongjie/scrapy-fingerprint)
    )
    
*   \[jxlil/scrapy-impersonate\]([https://github.com/jxlil/scrapy-impersonate](https://github.com/jxlil/scrapy-impersonate)
    )
    

Using with eventlet/gevent[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.3/community.html#using-with-eventlet-gevent "Link to this heading")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Just set `thread` to eventlet or gevent.

from curl\_cffi import requests

s \= requests.Session(thread\="eventlet")
s.get(url)

As a urllib3/requests adapter[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.3/community.html#as-a-urllib3-requests-adapter "Link to this heading")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You can also use curl-cffi as a requests adapter via [curl-adapter](https://github.com/el1s7/curl-adapter)
. In this way, you get the full functionality of requests.

import requests
from curl\_adapter import CurlCffiAdapter

session \= requests.Session()
session.mount("http://", CurlCffiAdapter())
session.mount("https://", CurlCffiAdapter())

\# just use requests session like you normally would
session.get("https://example.com")

As a httpx transport[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.3/community.html#as-a-httpx-transport "Link to this heading")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You can also use curl-cffi as a httpx transport via [httpx-curl-cffi](https://github.com/vgavro/httpx-curl-cffi)
. With this, you get the full functionality of httpx.

from httpx import Client, AsyncClient
from httpx\_curl\_cffi import CurlTransport, AsyncCurlTransport, CurlOpt

client \= Client(transport\=CurlTransport(impersonate\="chrome", default\_headers\=True))
client.get("https://tools.scrapfly.io/api/fp/ja3")

async\_client \= AsyncClient(transport\=AsyncCurlTransport(
    impersonate\="chrome",
    default\_headers\=True,
    \# required for parallel requests, see curl\_cffi issues below
    curl\_options\={CurlOpt.FRESH\_CONNECT: True}
))

# Cookies — curl_cffi  documentation

### Navigation

*   [index](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.3/genindex.html "General Index")
    
*   [next](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.3/community.html "Community")
     |
*   [previous](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.3/vs-requests.html "Compatibility with requests")
     |
*   [curl\_cffi documentation](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.3/index.html)
     »
*   [Cookies](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.3/cookies.html)
    

Cookies[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.3/cookies.html#cookies "Link to this heading")

====================================================================================================

Save and load cookies[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.3/cookies.html#save-and-load-cookies "Link to this heading")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Do not use `get_dict` to dump and load cookies. Cookies are more than just plain key-value pairs.

Using pickle:

\# example from: https://github.com/encode/httpx/issues/895
import pickle
\# import httpx
import curl\_cffi

def save\_cookies(client):
    with open("cookies.pk", "wb") as f:
        pickle.dump(client.cookies.jar.\_cookies, f)

def load\_cookies():
    if not os.path.isfile("cookies.pk"):
        return None
    with open("cookies.pk", "rb") as f:
        return pickle.load(f)

\# client = httpx.Client(cookies=load\_cookies())
client \= curl\_cffi.Session()
client.get("https://httpbin.org/cookies/set/foo/bar")
save\_cookies(client)

client \= curl\_cffi.Session()
client.cookies.jar.\_cookies.update(load\_cookies())
print(client.cookies.get("foo"))

Using mozilla cookie jar:

See: [https://github.com/lexiforest/curl\_cffi/issues/381](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl_cffi/issues/381)

TODO: expose libcurl’s native cookies.txt support.

Discard cookies when using Session[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.3/cookies.html#discard-cookies-when-using-session "Link to this heading")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You may need to discard cookies when using sessions, especially when using `AsyncSession`.

Use the `discard_cookies` option.

s \= requests.Session()

set\_url \= "https://httpbin.org/cookies"

r \= s.get(set\_url)
assert r.cookies\["foo"\] \== s.cookies\["foo"\]
old\_cookie \= r.cookies\["foo"\]

\# Let's start discarding cookies
s.discard\_cookies \= True
r \= s.get(set\_url)
assert r.cookies\["foo"\] != s.cookies\["foo"\]
assert old\_cookie \== s.cookies\["foo"\]

\# The behavior can be reverted
s.discard\_cookies \= False
r \= s.get(set\_url)
assert r.cookies\["foo"\] \== s.cookies\["foo"\]
old\_cookie \= r.cookies\["foo"\]

\# Also works as request parameter
r \= s.get(set\_url, discard\_cookies\=True)
assert r.cookies\["foo"\] != s.cookies\["foo"\]
assert old\_cookie \== s.cookies\["foo"\]

### [Table of Contents](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.3/index.html)

*   [Cookies](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.3/cookies.html#)
    *   [Save and load cookies](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.3/cookies.html#save-and-load-cookies)
        
    *   [Discard cookies when using Session](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.3/cookies.html#discard-cookies-when-using-session)

# Exceptions — curl_cffi  documentation

Exceptions[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.0/exceptions.html#exceptions "Link to this heading")

=============================================================================================================

We try to follow the requests exception hirearchy, however, some are missing, while some are added.

If an exception is marked as “not used”, please catch the base exception.

Exceptions[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.0/exceptions.html#id1 "Link to this heading")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

_class_ curl\_cffi.requests.exceptions.RequestException(_msg_, _code: [CurlECode](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.0/api.html#curl_cffi.CurlECode "curl_cffi.const.CurlECode")
 | Literal\[0\] \= 0_, _response\=None_, _\*args_, _\*\*kwargs_)[\[source\]](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.0/_modules/curl_cffi/requests/exceptions.html#RequestException)
[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.0/exceptions.html#curl_cffi.requests.exceptions.RequestException "Link to this definition")

Base exception for curl\_cffi.requests package

_class_ curl\_cffi.requests.exceptions.CookieConflict(_msg_, _code: [CurlECode](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.0/api.html#curl_cffi.CurlECode "curl_cffi.const.CurlECode")
 | Literal\[0\] \= 0_, _response\=None_, _\*args_, _\*\*kwargs_)[\[source\]](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.0/_modules/curl_cffi/requests/exceptions.html#CookieConflict)
[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.0/exceptions.html#curl_cffi.requests.exceptions.CookieConflict "Link to this definition")

Same cookie exists for different domains.

_class_ curl\_cffi.requests.exceptions.SessionClosed(_msg_, _code: [CurlECode](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.0/api.html#curl_cffi.CurlECode "curl_cffi.const.CurlECode")
 | Literal\[0\] \= 0_, _response\=None_, _\*args_, _\*\*kwargs_)[\[source\]](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.0/_modules/curl_cffi/requests/exceptions.html#SessionClosed)
[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.0/exceptions.html#curl_cffi.requests.exceptions.SessionClosed "Link to this definition")

The session has already been closed.

_class_ curl\_cffi.requests.exceptions.ImpersonateError(_msg_, _code: [CurlECode](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.0/api.html#curl_cffi.CurlECode "curl_cffi.const.CurlECode")
 | Literal\[0\] \= 0_, _response\=None_, _\*args_, _\*\*kwargs_)[\[source\]](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.0/_modules/curl_cffi/requests/exceptions.html#ImpersonateError)
[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.0/exceptions.html#curl_cffi.requests.exceptions.ImpersonateError "Link to this definition")

The impersonate config was wrong or impersonate failed.

_class_ curl\_cffi.requests.exceptions.InvalidJSONError(_msg_, _code: [CurlECode](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.0/api.html#curl_cffi.CurlECode "curl_cffi.const.CurlECode")
 | Literal\[0\] \= 0_, _response\=None_, _\*args_, _\*\*kwargs_)[\[source\]](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.0/_modules/curl_cffi/requests/exceptions.html#InvalidJSONError)
[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.0/exceptions.html#curl_cffi.requests.exceptions.InvalidJSONError "Link to this definition")

A JSON error occurred. not used

_class_ curl\_cffi.requests.exceptions.HTTPError(_msg_, _code: [CurlECode](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.0/api.html#curl_cffi.CurlECode "curl_cffi.const.CurlECode")
 | Literal\[0\] \= 0_, _response\=None_, _\*args_, _\*\*kwargs_)[\[source\]](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.0/_modules/curl_cffi/requests/exceptions.html#HTTPError)
[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.0/exceptions.html#curl_cffi.requests.exceptions.HTTPError "Link to this definition")

An HTTP error occurred.

_class_ curl\_cffi.requests.exceptions.IncompleteRead(_msg_, _code: [CurlECode](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.0/api.html#curl_cffi.CurlECode "curl_cffi.const.CurlECode")
 | Literal\[0\] \= 0_, _response\=None_, _\*args_, _\*\*kwargs_)[\[source\]](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.0/_modules/curl_cffi/requests/exceptions.html#IncompleteRead)
[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.0/exceptions.html#curl_cffi.requests.exceptions.IncompleteRead "Link to this definition")

Incomplete read of content

_class_ curl\_cffi.requests.exceptions.ConnectionError(_msg_, _code: [CurlECode](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.0/api.html#curl_cffi.CurlECode "curl_cffi.const.CurlECode")
 | Literal\[0\] \= 0_, _response\=None_, _\*args_, _\*\*kwargs_)[\[source\]](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.0/_modules/curl_cffi/requests/exceptions.html#ConnectionError)
[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.0/exceptions.html#curl_cffi.requests.exceptions.ConnectionError "Link to this definition")

# TLS PSK(41) Extension — curl_cffi  documentation

### Navigation

*   [index](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.13.0/genindex.html "General Index")
    
*   [next](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.13.0/impersonate/faq.html "Impersonation FAQ")
     |
*   [previous](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.13.0/impersonate/customize.html "How to use my own fingerprints? e.g. okhttp")
     |
*   [curl\_cffi documentation](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.13.0/index.html)
     »
*   [Impersonate Guide](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.13.0/impersonate/_index.html)
     »
*   [TLS PSK(41) Extension](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.13.0/impersonate/psk.html)
    

TLS PSK(41) Extension[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.13.0/impersonate/psk.html#tls-psk-41-extension "Link to this heading")

=======================================================================================================================================

What is the TLS PSK(41) extension, how to deal with it?

PSK is short for `Pre-Shared Key`, as defined in [RFC 8446](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446.html#section-2.2)
,

> Once a handshake has completed, the server can send the client a PSK identity that corresponds to a unique key derived from the initial handshake (see Section 4.6.1). The client can then use that PSK identity in future handshakes to negotiate the use of the associated PSK.

Usually, when you first visit a website, the PSK extension is not present in the extension list. But when you visit the same website for the second time, in a relatively short time, the client may offer a PSK extension with the key from the server.

For example, you can visit `https://tls.peet.ws/api/all`, and then refresh the page, the PSK extension will be there.

To correctly implement the PSK extension, the client must have some kind of a session cache held in memory or persisted on disk. All the major browsers have this feature for a very long time. `curl_cffi` added this feature in version `0.11.0`, with libcurl `8.13.0`.

The mechanism and behavior of a PSK looks like an http session cookie, where the server sent a cryptographic value as a key to resume a previous disconnected session. When the server generates a PSK, it is possible that the server keeps the mapping between the incoming IP and key. Thus, it can be problematic if you reuse a TLS session with rotating proxies.

┌───────────┐                                    ┌───────────┐
│           │                                    │           │
│           │             IP: 10.0.0.1           │           │
│           ┼─────────────TLS─Hello──────────────►           │
│           │                                    │           │
│           ◄─────────────PSK:─xxx───────────────┼           │
│           │                                    │           │
│           │                                    │           │
│           │                                    │           │
│           │                                    │  Server   │
│  Client   │             IP: 10.0.0.2           │           │
│           ┼─────────────TLS─with─PSK───────────►           │
│           │                                    │           │
│           ◄─────────────Blocked────────────────┼           │
│           │                                    │           │
│           │             PSK: xxx was           │           │
│           │             associated with        │           │
│           │             10.0.0.1, not          │           │
└───────────┘             10.0.0.2               └───────────┘

Luckily, since curl\_cffi `0.12.0`, we added a new option called `proxy_credential_no_reuse`, when enabled, the TLS session cache will be bound based on the proxy username and IP, such that the session can only be reused when the proxy username and IP matches. From the server’s viewpoint, the `Pre-Shared Key` will be locked to the same source IP, not bouncing around among different exit nodes.

\# Python example to be added.
\# We might enable this by default when proxies are used.

How do I enable PSK extension anyway?[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.13.0/impersonate/psk.html#how-do-i-enable-psk-extension-anyway "Link to this heading")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You don’t. If you haven’t, please read the explanation above first. Generally speaking, the client should manage this extension, and it should automatically offer this extension on the second request.

From the server’s perspective, if you forcefully add a PSK extension with random value, it’s an obvious sign that you are not a valid visitor, just like you providing an invalid cookie value.

However, it’s reasonable that you don’t want the PSK extension to be sent, i.e. pretending to be a first time visitor. We don’t support this for now, your option is to use an older version of curl\_cffi or create a new session on each request.

Note, some other impersonation-oriented http clients give you the control over adding the PSK or not, but you should let the client decide, if you are trying to impersonating browsers.

*   [TLS PSK(41) Extension](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.13.0/impersonate/psk.html#)
    *   [How do I enable PSK extension anyway?](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.13.0/impersonate/psk.html#how-do-i-enable-psk-extension-anyway)

# How to use my own fingerprints? e.g. okhttp — curl_cffi  documentation

### Navigation

*   [index](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.12.0/genindex.html "General Index")
    
*   [next](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.12.0/impersonate/psk.html "TLS PSK(41) Extension")
     |
*   [previous](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.12.0/impersonate/targets.html "Supported browser impersonate targets")
     |
*   [curl\_cffi documentation](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.12.0/index.html)
     »
*   [Impersonate Guide](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.12.0/impersonate/_index.html)
     »
*   [How to use my own fingerprints? e.g. okhttp](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.12.0/impersonate/customize.html)
    

How to use my own fingerprints? e.g. okhttp[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.12.0/impersonate/customize.html#how-to-use-my-own-fingerprints-e-g-okhttp "Link to this heading")

========================================================================================================================================================================================

Use `ja3=...`, `akamai=...` and `extra_fp=...`.

You can retrieve the JA3 and Akamai strings using tools like WireShark or from TLS fingerprinting sites.

\# OKHTTP impersonatation examples
\# credits: https://github.com/bogdanfinn/tls-client/blob/master/profiles/contributed\_custom\_profiles.go

url \= "https://tls.browserleaks.com/json"

okhttp4\_android10\_ja3 \= ",".join(
    \[\
        "771",\
        "4865-4866-4867-49195-49196-52393-49199-49200-52392-49171-49172-156-157-47-53",\
        "0-23-65281-10-11-35-16-5-13-51-45-43-21",\
        "29-23-24",\
        "0",\
    \]
)

okhttp4\_android10\_akamai \= "4:16777216|16711681|0|m,p,a,s"

extra\_fp \= {
    "tls\_signature\_algorithms": \[\
        "ecdsa\_secp256r1\_sha256",\
        "rsa\_pss\_rsae\_sha256",\
        "rsa\_pkcs1\_sha256",\
        "ecdsa\_secp384r1\_sha384",\
        "rsa\_pss\_rsae\_sha384",\
        "rsa\_pkcs1\_sha384",\
        "rsa\_pss\_rsae\_sha512",\
        "rsa\_pkcs1\_sha512",\
        "rsa\_pkcs1\_sha1",\
    \]
    \# other options:
    \# tls\_min\_version: int = CurlSslVersion.TLSv1\_2
    \# tls\_grease: bool = False
    \# tls\_permute\_extensions: bool = False
    \# tls\_cert\_compression: Literal\["zlib", "brotli"\] = "brotli"
    \# tls\_signature\_algorithms: Optional\[List\[str\]\] = None
    \# http2\_stream\_weight: int = 256
    \# http2\_stream\_exclusive: int = 1

    \# See requests/impersonate.py and tests/unittest/test\_impersonate.py for more examples
}

r \= curl\_cffi.get(
    url, ja3\=okhttp4\_android10\_ja3, akamai\=okhttp4\_android10\_akamai, extra\_fp\=extra\_fp
)
print(r.json())

JA3 and Akamai String Format[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.12.0/impersonate/customize.html#ja3-and-akamai-string-format "Link to this heading")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

A JA3 string is a simple, comma-separated representation of the key fields in a TLS ClientHello. It consists of five parts:

*   SSL/TLS Version, The numeric version the client requests (e.g. 771 for TLS 1.2).
    
*   Cipher Suites, A hyphen-separated list of all cipher suite IDs offered by the client (e.g. 4865-4866-4867-49195-49196).
    
*   Extension IDs, A hyphen-separated list of all TLS extension numbers the client includes (e.g. 0-11-10-35-16-5).
    
*   Supported Groups (aka “Elliptic Curves”), A hyphen-separated list of curve IDs the client supports for ECDHE (e.g. 29-23-24).
    
*   EC Point Formats, A hyphen-separated list of the point‐format IDs (almost always just 0 for “uncompressed”) (e.g. 0).
    

They’re concatenated in that exact order, with commas between fields. For example:

771,4865\-4866\-4867\-49195\-49196,0\-11\-10\-35\-16\-5,29\-23\-24,0

Note that Chrome permutes the extension order on each request, so there is a new format called JA3N, which uses sorted extension\_id list.

The Akamai HTTP/2 fingerprint string encodes four client‐controlled protocol parameters, joined by the pipe character (|):

*   SETTINGS, A semicolon‐separated list of ID:value pairs from the client’s initial SETTINGS frame. Each ID is a standard HTTP/2 setting identifier (e.g. 1 for HEADER\_TABLE\_SIZE, 4 for INITIAL\_WINDOW\_SIZE), and value is the client’s chosen value for that setting
    
*   WINDOW\_UPDATE, A single integer: the value the client sends in its first WINDOW\_UPDATE frame (or 0 if none was sent)
    
*   PRIORITY, Zero or more priority‐frame tuples, each formatted as `StreamID:ExclusiveBit:DependentStreamID:Weight`. Multiple tuples are comma-separated. This captures any PRIORITY frames the client issues before sending headers
    
*   Pseudo-Header Order, The sequence in which the client sends HTTP/2 pseudo-headers in its request HEADERS frame, encoded as comma-separated single-letter codes:
    

Putting it all together, an example fingerprint might look like:

1:65536;4:131072;5:16384|12517377|3:0:0:201|m,p,a,s

where:

SETTINGS \= 1:65536;4:131072;5:16384
WINDOW\_UPDATE \= 12517377
PRIORITY \= 3:0:0:201
Pseudo\-Header Order \= m,p,a,s

Although JA3 and Akamai fingerprint string already captures many of the aspects of a Hello Packet, there are still some fields are not covered and can be used to detect you. This is when the `extra_fp` option comes in, each field of this dict is pretty easy to understand. You should first set the ja3 and akamai string, then check if you have the identical fingerprint like your target. If not, use the `extra_fp` to further refine your impersonation.

Using CURLOPTs[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.12.0/impersonate/customize.html#using-curlopts "Link to this heading")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The other way is to use the `curlopt` s to specify exactly which options you want to change.

To modify them, use `curl.setopt(CurlOpt, value)`, for example:

import curl\_cffi
from curl\_cffi import Curl, CurlOpt

c \= Curl()
c.setopt(CurlOpt.HTTP2\_PSEUDO\_HEADERS\_ORDER, "masp")

\# or
curl\_cffi.get(url, curl\_options\={CurlOpt.HTTP2\_PSEUDO\_HEADERS\_ORDER, "masp"})

Here are a list of options:

For TLS/JA3 fingerprints:

*   [https://curl.se/libcurl/c/CURLOPT\_SSL\_CIPHER\_LIST.html](https://curl.se/libcurl/c/CURLOPT_SSL_CIPHER_LIST.html)
    

and non-standard TLS options created for this project:

*   `CURLOPT_SSL_ENABLE_ALPS`
    
*   `CURLOPT_SSL_SIG_HASH_ALGS`
    
*   `CURLOPT_SSL_CERT_COMPRESSION`
    
*   `CURLOPT_SSL_ENABLE_TICKET`
    
*   `CURLOPT_SSL_PERMUTE_EXTENSIONS`
    

For Akamai http2 fingerprints, you can fully customize the 3 parts:

*   `CURLOPT_HTTP2_PSEUDO_HEADERS_ORDER`, sets http2 pseudo header order, for example: `masp` (non-standard HTTP/2 options created for this project).
    
*   `CURLOPT_HTTP2_SETTINGS` sets the settings frame values, for example `1:65536;3:1000;4:6291456;6:262144` (non-standard HTTP/2 options created for this project).
    
*   `CURLOPT_HTTP2_WINDOW_UPDATE` sets initial window update value for http2, for example `15663105` (non-standard HTTP/2 options created for this project).
    

For a complete list of options and explanation, see the [\`curl-impersoante README\`\_](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.12.0/impersonate/customize.html#id1)
.

How to toggle firefox-specific extensions?[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.12.0/impersonate/customize.html#how-to-toggle-firefox-specific-extensions "Link to this heading")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

There are a few special extensions from firefox that you should add extra options by `extra_fp`:

Extension 34: delegated credentials

extra\_fp \= {
    "tls\_delegated\_credential": "ecdsa\_secp256r1\_sha256:ecdsa\_secp384r1\_sha384:ecdsa\_secp521r1\_sha512:ecdsa\_sha1"
}

\# Note that the ja3 string also includes extensiion: 34
ja3 \= "771,4865-4867-4866-49195-49199-52393-52392-49196-49200-49162-49161-49171-49172-156-157-47-53,0-23-65281-10-11-35-16-5-34-18-51-43-13-45-28-27-65037,4588-29-23-24-25-256-257,0"

r \= curl\_cffi.get(url, ja3\=ja3, extra\_fp\=extra\_fp)

Extension 28: record size limit

extra\_fp \= {
    "tls\_record\_size\_limit": 4001
}

\# Note that the ja3 string also includes extensiion: 28
ja3 \= "771,4865-4867-4866-49195-49199-52393-52392-49196-49200-49162-49161-49171-49172-156-157-47-53,0-23-65281-10-11-35-16-5-34-18-51-43-13-45-28-27-65037,4588-29-23-24-25-256-257,0"

r \= curl\_cffi.get(url, ja3\=ja3, extra\_fp\=extra\_fp)

# Exceptions — curl_cffi  documentation

Exceptions[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.2/exceptions.html#exceptions "Link to this heading")

=============================================================================================================

We try to follow the requests exception hirearchy, however, some are missing, while some are added.

If an exception is marked as “not used”, please catch the base exception.

Exceptions[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.2/exceptions.html#id1 "Link to this heading")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Cookies — curl_cffi  documentation

### Navigation

*   [index](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.4/genindex.html "General Index")
    
*   [next](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.4/community.html "Community")
     |
*   [previous](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.4/vs-requests.html "Compatibility with requests")
     |
*   [curl\_cffi documentation](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.4/index.html)
     »
*   [Cookies](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.4/cookies.html)
    

Cookies[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.4/cookies.html#cookies "Link to this heading")

====================================================================================================

Save and load cookies[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.4/cookies.html#save-and-load-cookies "Link to this heading")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Do not use `get_dict` to dump and load cookies. Cookies are more than just plain key-value pairs.

Using pickle:

\# example from: https://github.com/encode/httpx/issues/895
import pickle
\# import httpx
import curl\_cffi

def save\_cookies(client):
    with open("cookies.pk", "wb") as f:
        pickle.dump(client.cookies.jar.\_cookies, f)

def load\_cookies():
    if not os.path.isfile("cookies.pk"):
        return None
    with open("cookies.pk", "rb") as f:
        return pickle.load(f)

\# client = httpx.Client(cookies=load\_cookies())
client \= curl\_cffi.Session()
client.get("https://httpbin.org/cookies/set/foo/bar")
save\_cookies(client)

client \= curl\_cffi.Session()
client.cookies.jar.\_cookies.update(load\_cookies())
print(client.cookies.get("foo"))

Using mozilla cookie jar:

See: [https://github.com/lexiforest/curl\_cffi/issues/381](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl_cffi/issues/381)

TODO: expose libcurl’s native cookies.txt support.

Discard cookies when using Session[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.4/cookies.html#discard-cookies-when-using-session "Link to this heading")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You may need to discard cookies when using sessions, especially when using `AsyncSession`.

Use the `discard_cookies` option.

s \= requests.Session()

set\_url \= "https://httpbin.org/cookies"

r \= s.get(set\_url)
assert r.cookies\["foo"\] \== s.cookies\["foo"\]
old\_cookie \= r.cookies\["foo"\]

\# Let's start discarding cookies
s.discard\_cookies \= True
r \= s.get(set\_url)
assert r.cookies\["foo"\] != s.cookies\["foo"\]
assert old\_cookie \== s.cookies\["foo"\]

\# The behavior can be reverted
s.discard\_cookies \= False
r \= s.get(set\_url)
assert r.cookies\["foo"\] \== s.cookies\["foo"\]
old\_cookie \= r.cookies\["foo"\]

\# Also works as request parameter
r \= s.get(set\_url, discard\_cookies\=True)
assert r.cookies\["foo"\] != s.cookies\["foo"\]
assert old\_cookie \== s.cookies\["foo"\]

    *   [Discard cookies when using Session](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.4/cookies.html#discard-cookies-when-using-session)

# Community — curl_cffi  documentation

### Navigation

     |

     |
*   [curl\_cffi documentation](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.4/index.html)
     »
*   [Community](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.4/community.html)
    

Community[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.4/community.html#community "Link to this heading")

==========================================================================================================

Scrapy integrations[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.4/community.html#scrapy-integrations "Link to this heading")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

If you are using scrapy, check out these middlewares:

*   \[tieyongjie/scrapy-fingerprint\]([https://github.com/tieyongjie/scrapy-fingerprint](https://github.com/tieyongjie/scrapy-fingerprint)
    )
    
*   \[jxlil/scrapy-impersonate\]([https://github.com/jxlil/scrapy-impersonate](https://github.com/jxlil/scrapy-impersonate)
    )
    

Using with eventlet/gevent[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.4/community.html#using-with-eventlet-gevent "Link to this heading")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Just set `thread` to eventlet or gevent.

from curl\_cffi import requests

s \= requests.Session(thread\="eventlet")
s.get(url)

As a urllib3/requests adapter[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.4/community.html#as-a-urllib3-requests-adapter "Link to this heading")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You can also use curl-cffi as a requests adapter via [curl-adapter](https://github.com/el1s7/curl-adapter)
. In this way, you get the full functionality of requests.

import requests
from curl\_adapter import CurlCffiAdapter

session \= requests.Session()
session.mount("http://", CurlCffiAdapter())
session.mount("https://", CurlCffiAdapter())

\# just use requests session like you normally would
session.get("https://example.com")

As a httpx transport[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.4/community.html#as-a-httpx-transport "Link to this heading")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You can also use curl-cffi as a httpx transport via [httpx-curl-cffi](https://github.com/vgavro/httpx-curl-cffi)
. With this, you get the full functionality of httpx.

from httpx import Client, AsyncClient
from httpx\_curl\_cffi import CurlTransport, AsyncCurlTransport, CurlOpt

client \= Client(transport\=CurlTransport(impersonate\="chrome", default\_headers\=True))
client.get("https://tools.scrapfly.io/api/fp/ja3")

async\_client \= AsyncClient(transport\=AsyncCurlTransport(
    impersonate\="chrome",
    default\_headers\=True,
    \# required for parallel requests, see curl\_cffi issues below
    curl\_options\={CurlOpt.FRESH\_CONNECT: True}
))

# Compatibility with requests — curl_cffi  documentation

Compatibility with requests[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.1/vs-requests.html#compatibility-with-requests "Link to this heading")

================================================================================================================================================

Although we try our best to mimic the requests API, some functionality is not easy to implement and left out. Here are a list of known incompatibilities:

*   files API are slightly different, but more error-proof.
    
*   retries are not supported yet, tracked in \[#24\]([https://github.com/lexiforest/curl\_cffi/issues/24](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl_cffi/issues/24)
    )
    
*   redirect history are not supported, tracked in \[#82\]([https://github.com/lexiforest/curl\_cffi/issues/82](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl_cffi/issues/82)
    )
    
*   empty-domains cookies may lost during redirects, tracked in \[#55\]([https://github.com/lexiforest/curl\_cffi/issues/55](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl_cffi/issues/55)
    )
    
*   response object can not be pickled.
    

Alternatively, you can also use curl-cffi as a requests adapter via [curl-adapter](https://github.com/el1s7/curl-adapter)
. In this way, you get the full functionality of requests.

[curl\_cffi](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.1/index.html)

=====================================================================
