# TLS PSK(41) Extension — curl_cffi  documentation

### Navigation

*   [index](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.12.0/genindex.html "General Index")
    
*   [next](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.12.0/impersonate/faq.html "Impersonation FAQ")
     |
*   [previous](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.12.0/impersonate/customize.html "How to use my own fingerprints? e.g. okhttp")
     |
*   [curl\_cffi documentation](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.12.0/index.html)
     »
*   [Impersonate Guide](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.12.0/impersonate/_index.html)
     »
*   [TLS PSK(41) Extension](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.12.0/impersonate/psk.html)
    

TLS PSK(41) Extension[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.12.0/impersonate/psk.html#tls-psk-41-extension "Link to this heading")

=======================================================================================================================================

What is the TLS PSK(41) extension, how to deal with it?

PSK is short for `Pre-Shared Key`, as defined in [RFC 8446](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446.html#section-2.2)
,

> Once a handshake has completed, the server can send the client a PSK identity that corresponds to a unique key derived from the initial handshake (see Section 4.6.1). The client can then use that PSK identity in future handshakes to negotiate the use of the associated PSK.

Usually, when you first visit a website, the PSK extension is not present in the extension list. But when you visit the same website for the second time, in a relatively short time, the client may offer a PSK extension with the key from the server.

For example, you can visit `https://tls.peet.ws/api/all`, and then refresh the page, the PSK extension will be there.

To correctly implement the PSK extension, the client must have some kind of a session cache held in memory or persisted on disk. All the major browsers have this feature for a very long time. `curl_cffi` added this feature in version `0.11.0`, with libcurl `8.13.0`.

The mechanism and behavior of a PSK looks like an http session cookie, where the server sent a cryptographic value as a key to resume a previous disconnected session. When the server generates a PSK, it is possible that the server keeps the mapping between the incoming IP and key. Thus, it can be problematic if you reuse a TLS session with rotating proxies.

┌───────────┐                                    ┌───────────┐
│           │                                    │           │
│           │             IP: 10.0.0.1           │           │
│           ┼─────────────TLS─Hello──────────────►           │
│           │                                    │           │
│           ◄─────────────PSK:─xxx───────────────┼           │
│           │                                    │           │
│           │                                    │           │
│           │                                    │           │
│           │                                    │  Server   │
│  Client   │             IP: 10.0.0.2           │           │
│           ┼─────────────TLS─with─PSK───────────►           │
│           │                                    │           │
│           ◄─────────────Blocked────────────────┼           │
│           │                                    │           │
│           │             PSK: xxx was           │           │
│           │             associated with        │           │
│           │             10.0.0.1, not          │           │
└───────────┘             10.0.0.2               └───────────┘

Luckily, since curl\_cffi `0.12.0`, we added a new option called `proxy_credential_no_reuse`, when enabled, the TLS session cache will be bound based on the proxy username and IP, such that the session can only be reused when the proxy username and IP matches. From the server’s viewpoint, the `Pre-Shared Key` will be locked to the same source IP, not bouncing around among different exit nodes.

\# Python example to be added.
\# We might enable this by default when proxies are used.

How do I enable PSK extension anyway?[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.12.0/impersonate/psk.html#how-do-i-enable-psk-extension-anyway "Link to this heading")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You don’t. If you haven’t, please read the explanation above first. Generally speaking, the client should manage this extension, and it should automatically offer this extension on the second request.

From the server’s perspective, if you forcefully add a PSK extension with random value, it’s an obvious sign that you are not a valid visitor, just like you providing an invalid cookie value.

However, it’s reasonable that you don’t want the PSK extension to be sent, i.e. pretending to be a first time visitor. We don’t support this for now, your option is to use an older version of curl\_cffi or create a new session on each request.

Note, some other impersonation-oriented http clients give you the control over adding the PSK or not, but you should let the client decide, if you are trying to impersonating browsers.

### [Table of Contents](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.12.0/index.html)

*   [TLS PSK(41) Extension](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.12.0/impersonate/psk.html#)
    *   [How do I enable PSK extension anyway?](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.12.0/impersonate/psk.html#how-do-i-enable-psk-extension-anyway)

# Exceptions — curl_cffi  documentation

Exceptions[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.10.0/exceptions.html#exceptions "Link to this heading")

=============================================================================================================

We try to follow the requests exception hirearchy, however, some are missing, while some are added.

If an exception is marked as “not used”, please catch the base exception.

Exceptions[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.10.0/exceptions.html#id1 "Link to this heading")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Exceptions — curl_cffi  documentation

Exceptions[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.1/exceptions.html#exceptions "Link to this heading")

=============================================================================================================

We try to follow the requests exception hirearchy, however, some are missing, while some are added.

If an exception is marked as “not used”, please catch the base exception.

Exceptions[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.1/exceptions.html#id1 "Link to this heading")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Impersonation FAQ[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.13.0/impersonate/faq.html#impersonation-faq "Link to this heading")

================================================================================================================================

How to check if my impersonation is working?[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.13.0/impersonate/faq.html#how-to-check-if-my-impersonation-is-working "Link to this heading")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The most reliable way is to use WireShark, and compare packets from `curl_cffi` and your targets.

If it’s challenging for you to use WireShark, you can use the following sites for JA3 and Akamai fingerprints:

1.  [https://tls.browserleaks.com/json](https://tls.browserleaks.com/json)
    
2.  [https://tls.peet.ws/api/all](https://tls.peet.ws/api/all)
    
3.  [https://scrapfly.io/web-scraping-tools/browser-fingerprint](https://scrapfly.io/web-scraping-tools/browser-fingerprint)
    

For http/3 fingerprints, use our service:

1.  [https://fp.impersonate.pro/api/http3](https://fp.impersonate.pro/api/http3)
    

I’m still being detected even if I impersonated correctly[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.13.0/impersonate/faq.html#i-m-still-being-detected-even-if-i-impersonated-correctly "Link to this heading")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

First, JA3 and akamai fingerprints are not comprehensive, there are other fields that can be detected, we have a few more options listed in `extra_fp`. Be sure to also check them.

Note

Since `curl-impersonate` was posted on [Hacker News](https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=42547820)
, some features and behaviors of `curl_cffi` is being detected by professional players. If we continue to fix these niche behavior in public, it would soon be noticed by those providers.

In short, if you are using curl\_cffi in production and you are sure about being blocked by TLS or http detection, try the [curl\_cffi pro version](https://impersonate.pro/)
.

Should I randomize my fingerprints for each request?[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.13.0/impersonate/faq.html#should-i-randomize-my-fingerprints-for-each-request "Link to this heading")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You can choose a random version from the list above, like:

random.choice(\["chrome119", "chrome120", ...\])

However, be aware of the browser market share, very old versions are not good choices.

Generally, you should not try to generate a customized random fingerprints. The reason is that, for a given browser version, the fingerprints are fixed. If you create a new random fingerprints, the server is easy to know that you are not using a typical browser.

If you were thinking about `ja3`, and not `ja3n`, then the fingerprints is already randomized, due to the `extension permutation` feature introduced in Chrome 110.

As far as we know, most websites use an allowlist, not a blocklist to filter out bot traffic. So do not expect random ja3 fingerprints would work in the wild.

Moreover, do not generate random ja3 strings. There are certain limits for a valid ja3 string. For example:

*   TLS 1.3 ciphers must be at the front.
    
*   GREASE extension must be the first.
    
*   etc.
    

You should copy ja3 strings from sniffing tools, not generate them, unless you can make sure all the requirements are met.

Can I change JavaScript fingerprints with this library?[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.13.0/impersonate/faq.html#can-i-change-javascript-fingerprints-with-this-library "Link to this heading")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

No, you can not. As the name suggests, JavaScript fingerprints are generated using JavaScript APIs provided by real browsers. `curl_cffi` is a python binding to a C library, with no browser or JavaScript runtime under the hood.

If you need to impersonate browsers on the JavaScript perspective, you can search for “Anti-detect Browser”, “Playwright stealth” and similar keywords. Or simply use a commercial plan from our sponsors.

Why are all the User-Agents macOS?[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.13.0/impersonate/faq.html#why-are-all-the-user-agents-macos "Link to this heading")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Simple, because I primarily use macOS and I copied the headers from my own browser. Fingerprints are generally the same across desktop OSes, if you want it to look like Windows, just update the user-agent and other related headers to Windows.

# FAQ — curl_cffi  documentation

FAQ[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.2/faq.html#faq "Link to this heading")

=======================================================================================

Why does the JA3 fingerprints change for Chrome 110+ impersonation?[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.2/faq.html#why-does-the-ja3-fingerprints-change-for-chrome-110-impersonation "Link to this heading")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This is intended.

Chrome introduces `ClientHello` permutation in version 110, which means the order of extensions will be random, thus JA3 fingerprints will be random. So, when comparing JA3 fingerprints of `curl_cffi` and a browser, they may differ. However, this does not mean that TLS fingerprints will not be a problem, `ClientHello` extension order is just one factor of how servers can tell automated requests from browsers.

Roughly, this can be mitigated like:

ja3 \= md5(list(extensions), ...other arguments)
ja3n \= md5(set(extensions), ...other arguments)

See more from [this article](https://www.fastly.com/blog/a-first-look-at-chromes-tls-clienthello-permutation-in-the-wild)
 and [curl-impersonate notes](https://github.com/lwthiker/curl-impersonate/pull/148)
.

Can I bypass Cloudflare with this project? or any other specific site.[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.2/faq.html#can-i-bypass-cloudflare-with-this-project-or-any-other-specific-site "Link to this heading")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Short answer is: it depends.

TLS and http2 fingerprints are just one of the many factors Cloudflare considers. Other factors include but are not limited to: IP quality, request rate, JS fingerprints, etc.

There are different protection levels for website owners to choose. For the most basic ones, TLS fingerprints alone maybe enough, but for higher levels, you may need to find a better proxy IP provider and use browser automation tools like playwright.

If you are in a hurry or just want the professionals to take care of the hard parts, you can consider the commercial solutions from our sponsors:

*   [Scrapfly](https://scrapfly.io/?utm_source=github&utm_medium=sponsoring&utm_campaign=curl_cffi)
    , Cloud-based scraping platform.
    
*   [Yescaptcha](https://yescaptcha.com/i/stfnIO)
    , captcha resolver and proxy service for bypassing Cloudflare.
    
*   [ScrapeNinja](https://scrapeninja.net/?utm_source=github&utm_medium=banner&utm_campaign=cffi)
    , Managed web scraping API.
    

For details, see the Sponsor section on front page.

I’m getting certs errors[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.2/faq.html#i-m-getting-certs-errors "Link to this heading")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The simplest way is to turn off cert verification by `verify=False`:

r \= requests.get("https://example.com", verify\=False)

ErrCode: 77, Reason: error setting certificate verify locations[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.2/faq.html#errcode-77-reason-error-setting-certificate-verify-locations "Link to this heading")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Install `curl_cffi` and its dependencies in a pure-ASCII path, i.e. no Chinese or any other characters out of the ASCII table in the path.

How to use with fiddler/charles to intercept content[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.2/faq.html#how-to-use-with-fiddler-charles-to-intercept-content "Link to this heading")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Fiddler and Charles uses man-in-the-middle self-signed certs to intercept TLS traffic, to use with them, simply set `verify=False`.

ErrCode: 92, Reason: ‘HTTP/2 stream 0 was not closed cleanly: PROTOCOL\_ERROR (err 1)’[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.2/faq.html#errcode-92-reason-http-2-stream-0-was-not-closed-cleanly-protocol-error-err-1 "Link to this heading")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This error(http/2 stream 0) has been reported many times ever since curl\_cffi was published, but I still can not find a reproducible way to trigger it. Given that the majority users are behind proxies, the situation is even more difficult to deal with.

I’m even not sure it’s a bug introduced in libcurl, curl-impersonate or curl\_cffi, or it’s just a server error. Depending on your context, here are some general suggestions for you:

*   First, try removing the `Content-Length` header from you request.
    
*   Try to see if this error was caused by proxies, if so, use better proxies.
    
*   If it stops working after a while, maybe you’re just being blocked by, such as, Akamai.
    
*   Force http/1.1 mode. Some websites’ h2 implementation is simply broken.
    
*   See if the url works in your real browser.
    
*   Find a stable way to reproduce it, so we can finally fix, or at least bypass it.
    

To force curl to use http 1.1 only.

from curl\_cffi import requests, CurlHttpVersion

r \= requests.get("https://postman-echo.com", http\_version\=CurlHttpVersion.V1\_1)

Related issues:

*   [#19](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl_cffi/issues/19)
    ,
    
*   [#42](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl_cffi/issues/42)
    ,
    
*   [#79](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl_cffi/issues/79)
    ,
    
*   [#165](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl_cffi/issues/165)
    ,
    

Packaging with PyInstaller[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.2/faq.html#packaging-with-pyinstaller "Link to this heading")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

If you encountered any issue with PyInstaller, here are a list of options provided by the community:

Add the `--hidden-import` option.

pyinstaller \-F .\\example.py \--hidden\-import\=\_cffi\_backend \--collect\-all curl\_cffi

Add other paths:

pyinstaller \--noconfirm \--onefile \--console \\
    \--paths "C:/Users/Administrator/AppData/Local/Programs/Python/Python39" \\
    \--add\-data "C:/Users/Administrator/AppData/Local/Programs/Python/Python39/Lib/site-packages/curl\_cffi.libs/libcurl-cbb416caa1dd01638554eab3f38d682d.dll;." \\
    \--collect\-data "curl\_cffi" \\
    "C:/Users/Administrator/Desktop/test\_script.py"

See also:

*   [#5](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl_cffi/issues/5)
    
*   [#48](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl_cffi/issues/48)
    

How to set proxy?[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.2/faq.html#how-to-set-proxy "Link to this heading")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You can use the `proxy` parameter:

from curl\_cffi import requests

requests.get(url, proxy\="http://user:pass@example.com:3128")

You can also use the `http_proxy`, `https_proxy`, and `ws_proxy`, `wss_proxy` environment variables, respectively.

For explanation of differences between `http_proxy` and `https_proxy`, please see [#6](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl_cffi/issues/6)
.

How to change the order of headers?[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.2/faq.html#how-to-change-the-order-of-headers "Link to this heading")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

By default, setting `impersonate` parameter will bring the corresponding headers. If you want to change the order or use your own headers, you need to turn off that and bring your own headers.

requests.get(url, impersonate\="chrome", default\_headers\=False, headers\=...)

How to deal with encoding/decoding errors?

Use `chardet` or `cchardet`

\>>> from curl\_cffi import requests
\>>> r \= requests.get("https://example.com/messy\_codec.html")
\>>> import chardet
\>>> chardet.detect(r.content)
{'encoding': 'GB2312', 'confidence': 0.99, 'language': 'Chinese'}

Or use regex or lxml to parse the meta header:

<meta http\-equiv\="Content-Type" content\="text/html; charset=gbk" />

[curl\_cffi](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.2/index.html)

====================================================================

# FAQ — curl_cffi  documentation

FAQ[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.0/faq.html#faq "Link to this heading")

========================================================================================

Why does the JA3 fingerprints change for Chrome 110+ impersonation?[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.0/faq.html#why-does-the-ja3-fingerprints-change-for-chrome-110-impersonation "Link to this heading")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This is intended.

Chrome introduces `ClientHello` permutation in version 110, which means the order of extensions will be random, thus JA3 fingerprints will be random. So, when comparing JA3 fingerprints of `curl_cffi` and a browser, they may differ. However, this does not mean that TLS fingerprints will not be a problem, `ClientHello` extension order is just one factor of how servers can tell automated requests from browsers.

Roughly, this can be mitigated like:

ja3 \= md5(list(extensions), ...other arguments)
ja3n \= md5(set(extensions), ...other arguments)

See more from [this article](https://www.fastly.com/blog/a-first-look-at-chromes-tls-clienthello-permutation-in-the-wild)
 and [curl-impersonate notes](https://github.com/lwthiker/curl-impersonate/pull/148)
.

Can I bypass Cloudflare with this project? or any other specific site.[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.0/faq.html#can-i-bypass-cloudflare-with-this-project-or-any-other-specific-site "Link to this heading")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Short answer is: it depends.

TLS and http2 fingerprints are just one of the many factors Cloudflare considers. Other factors include but are not limited to: IP quality, request rate, JS fingerprints, etc.

There are different protection levels for website owners to choose. For the most basic ones, TLS fingerprints alone maybe enough, but for higher levels, you may need to find a better proxy IP provider and use browser automation tools like playwright.

If you are in a hurry or just want the professionals to take care of the hard parts, you can consider the commercial solutions from our sponsors:

*   [Yescaptcha](https://yescaptcha.com/i/stfnIO)
    , captcha resolver and proxy service for bypassing Cloudflare.
    
*   [ScrapeNinja](https://scrapeninja.net/?utm_source=github&utm_medium=banner&utm_campaign=cffi)
    , Managed web scraping API.
    

For details, see the Sponsor section on front page.

I’m getting certs errors[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.0/faq.html#i-m-getting-certs-errors "Link to this heading")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The simplest way is to turn off cert verification by `verify=False`:

r \= curl\_cffi.get("https://example.com", verify\=False)

ErrCode: 77, Reason: error setting certificate verify locations[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.0/faq.html#errcode-77-reason-error-setting-certificate-verify-locations "Link to this heading")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Install `curl_cffi` and its dependencies in a pure-ASCII path, i.e. no Chinese or any other characters out of the ASCII table in the path.

How to use with fiddler/charles to intercept content[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.0/faq.html#how-to-use-with-fiddler-charles-to-intercept-content "Link to this heading")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Fiddler and Charles uses man-in-the-middle self-signed certs to intercept TLS traffic, to use with them, simply set `verify=False`.

ErrCode: 92, Reason: ‘HTTP/2 stream 0 was not closed cleanly: PROTOCOL\_ERROR (err 1)’[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.0/faq.html#errcode-92-reason-http-2-stream-0-was-not-closed-cleanly-protocol-error-err-1 "Link to this heading")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This error(http/2 stream 0) has been reported many times ever since curl\_cffi was published, but I still can not find a reproducible way to trigger it. Given that the majority users are behind proxies, the situation is even more difficult to deal with.

I’m even not sure it’s a bug introduced in libcurl, curl-impersonate or curl\_cffi, or it’s just a server error. Depending on your context, here are some general suggestions for you:

*   First, try removing the `Content-Length` header from you request.
    
*   Try to see if this error was caused by proxies, if so, use better proxies.
    
*   If it stops working after a while, maybe you’re just being blocked by, such as, Akamai.
    
*   Force http/1.1 mode. Some websites’ h2 implementation is simply broken.
    
*   See if the url works in your real browser.
    
*   Find a stable way to reproduce it, so we can finally fix, or at least bypass it.
    

To force curl to use http 1.1 only.

import curl\_cffi

r \= curl\_cffi.get("https://postman-echo.com", http\_version\=curl\_cffi.CurlHttpVersion.V1\_1)

Related issues:

*   [#19](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl_cffi/issues/19)
    ,
    
*   [#42](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl_cffi/issues/42)
    ,
    
*   [#79](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl_cffi/issues/79)
    ,
    
*   [#165](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl_cffi/issues/165)
    ,
    

Packaging with PyInstaller[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.0/faq.html#packaging-with-pyinstaller "Link to this heading")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

If you encountered any issue with PyInstaller, here are a list of options provided by the community:

Add the `--hidden-import` option.

pyinstaller \-F .\\example.py \--hidden\-import\=\_cffi\_backend \--collect\-all curl\_cffi

Add other paths:

pyinstaller \--noconfirm \--onefile \--console \\
    \--paths "C:/Users/Administrator/AppData/Local/Programs/Python/Python39" \\
    \--add\-data "C:/Users/Administrator/AppData/Local/Programs/Python/Python39/Lib/site-packages/curl\_cffi.libs/libcurl-cbb416caa1dd01638554eab3f38d682d.dll;." \\
    \--collect\-data "curl\_cffi" \\
    "C:/Users/Administrator/Desktop/test\_script.py"

See also:

*   [#5](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl_cffi/issues/5)
    
*   [#48](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl_cffi/issues/48)
    

How to set proxy?[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.0/faq.html#how-to-set-proxy "Link to this heading")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You can use the `proxy` parameter:

import curl\_cffi

curl\_cffi.get(url, proxy\="http://user:pass@example.com:3128")

You can also use the `http_proxy`, `https_proxy`, and `ws_proxy`, `wss_proxy` environment variables, respectively.

For explanation of differences between `http_proxy` and `https_proxy`, please see [#6](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl_cffi/issues/6)
.

How to change the order of headers?[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.0/faq.html#how-to-change-the-order-of-headers "Link to this heading")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

By default, setting `impersonate` parameter will bring the corresponding headers. If you want to change the order or use your own headers, you need to turn off that and bring your own headers.

requests.get(url, impersonate\="chrome", default\_headers\=False, headers\=...)

How to deal with encoding/decoding errors?

Use `chardet` or `cchardet`

\>>> import curl\_cffi
\>>> r \= curl\_cffi.get("https://example.com/messy\_codec.html")
\>>> import chardet
\>>> chardet.detect(r.content)
{'encoding': 'GB2312', 'confidence': 0.99, 'language': 'Chinese'}

Or use regex or lxml to parse the meta header:

<meta http\-equiv\="Content-Type" content\="text/html; charset=gbk" />

[curl\_cffi](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.0/index.html)

=====================================================================

# FAQ — curl_cffi  documentation

FAQ[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.10.0/faq.html#faq "Link to this heading")

========================================================================================

Why does the JA3 fingerprints change for Chrome 110+ impersonation?[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.10.0/faq.html#why-does-the-ja3-fingerprints-change-for-chrome-110-impersonation "Link to this heading")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This is intended.

Chrome introduces `ClientHello` permutation in version 110, which means the order of extensions will be random, thus JA3 fingerprints will be random. So, when comparing JA3 fingerprints of `curl_cffi` and a browser, they may differ. However, this does not mean that TLS fingerprints will not be a problem, `ClientHello` extension order is just one factor of how servers can tell automated requests from browsers.

Roughly, this can be mitigated like:

ja3 \= md5(list(extensions), ...other arguments)
ja3n \= md5(set(extensions), ...other arguments)

See more from [this article](https://www.fastly.com/blog/a-first-look-at-chromes-tls-clienthello-permutation-in-the-wild)
 and [curl-impersonate notes](https://github.com/lwthiker/curl-impersonate/pull/148)
.

Can I bypass Cloudflare with this project? or any other specific site.[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.10.0/faq.html#can-i-bypass-cloudflare-with-this-project-or-any-other-specific-site "Link to this heading")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Short answer is: it depends.

TLS and http2 fingerprints are just one of the many factors Cloudflare considers. Other factors include but are not limited to: IP quality, request rate, JS fingerprints, etc.

There are different protection levels for website owners to choose. For the most basic ones, TLS fingerprints alone maybe enough, but for higher levels, you may need to find a better proxy IP provider and use browser automation tools like playwright.

If you are in a hurry or just want the professionals to take care of the hard parts, you can consider the commercial solutions from our sponsors:

*   [Yescaptcha](https://yescaptcha.com/i/stfnIO)
    , captcha resolver and proxy service for bypassing Cloudflare.
    
*   [ScrapeNinja](https://scrapeninja.net/?utm_source=github&utm_medium=banner&utm_campaign=cffi)
    , Managed web scraping API.
    

For details, see the Sponsor section on front page.

I’m getting certs errors[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.10.0/faq.html#i-m-getting-certs-errors "Link to this heading")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The simplest way is to turn off cert verification by `verify=False`:

r \= curl\_cffi.get("https://example.com", verify\=False)

ErrCode: 77, Reason: error setting certificate verify locations[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.10.0/faq.html#errcode-77-reason-error-setting-certificate-verify-locations "Link to this heading")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Install `curl_cffi` and its dependencies in a pure-ASCII path, i.e. no Chinese or any other characters out of the ASCII table in the path.

How to use with fiddler/charles to intercept content[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.10.0/faq.html#how-to-use-with-fiddler-charles-to-intercept-content "Link to this heading")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Fiddler and Charles uses man-in-the-middle self-signed certs to intercept TLS traffic, to use with them, simply set `verify=False`.

ErrCode: 92, Reason: ‘HTTP/2 stream 0 was not closed cleanly: PROTOCOL\_ERROR (err 1)’[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.10.0/faq.html#errcode-92-reason-http-2-stream-0-was-not-closed-cleanly-protocol-error-err-1 "Link to this heading")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This error(http/2 stream 0) has been reported many times ever since curl\_cffi was published, but I still can not find a reproducible way to trigger it. Given that the majority users are behind proxies, the situation is even more difficult to deal with.

I’m even not sure it’s a bug introduced in libcurl, curl-impersonate or curl\_cffi, or it’s just a server error. Depending on your context, here are some general suggestions for you:

*   First, try removing the `Content-Length` header from you request.
    
*   Try to see if this error was caused by proxies, if so, use better proxies.
    
*   If it stops working after a while, maybe you’re just being blocked by, such as, Akamai.
    
*   Force http/1.1 mode. Some websites’ h2 implementation is simply broken.
    
*   See if the url works in your real browser.
    
*   Find a stable way to reproduce it, so we can finally fix, or at least bypass it.
    

To force curl to use http 1.1 only.

import curl\_cffi

r \= curl\_cffi.get("https://postman-echo.com", http\_version\=curl\_cffi.CurlHttpVersion.V1\_1)

Related issues:

*   [#19](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl_cffi/issues/19)
    ,
    
*   [#42](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl_cffi/issues/42)
    ,
    
*   [#79](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl_cffi/issues/79)
    ,
    
*   [#165](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl_cffi/issues/165)
    ,
    

Packaging with PyInstaller[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.10.0/faq.html#packaging-with-pyinstaller "Link to this heading")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

If you encountered any issue with PyInstaller, here are a list of options provided by the community:

Add the `--hidden-import` option.

pyinstaller \-F .\\example.py \--hidden\-import\=\_cffi\_backend \--collect\-all curl\_cffi

Add other paths:

pyinstaller \--noconfirm \--onefile \--console \\
    \--paths "C:/Users/Administrator/AppData/Local/Programs/Python/Python39" \\
    \--add\-data "C:/Users/Administrator/AppData/Local/Programs/Python/Python39/Lib/site-packages/curl\_cffi.libs/libcurl-cbb416caa1dd01638554eab3f38d682d.dll;." \\
    \--collect\-data "curl\_cffi" \\
    "C:/Users/Administrator/Desktop/test\_script.py"

See also:

*   [#5](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl_cffi/issues/5)
    
*   [#48](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl_cffi/issues/48)
    

How to set proxy?[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.10.0/faq.html#how-to-set-proxy "Link to this heading")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You can use the `proxy` parameter:

import curl\_cffi

curl\_cffi.get(url, proxy\="http://user:pass@example.com:3128")

You can also use the `http_proxy`, `https_proxy`, and `ws_proxy`, `wss_proxy` environment variables, respectively.

For explanation of differences between `http_proxy` and `https_proxy`, please see [#6](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl_cffi/issues/6)
.

How to change the order of headers?[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.10.0/faq.html#how-to-change-the-order-of-headers "Link to this heading")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

By default, setting `impersonate` parameter will bring the corresponding headers. If you want to change the order or use your own headers, you need to turn off that and bring your own headers.

requests.get(url, impersonate\="chrome", default\_headers\=False, headers\=...)

How to deal with encoding/decoding errors?

Use `chardet` or `cchardet`

\>>> import curl\_cffi
\>>> r \= curl\_cffi.get("https://example.com/messy\_codec.html")
\>>> import chardet
\>>> chardet.detect(r.content)
{'encoding': 'GB2312', 'confidence': 0.99, 'language': 'Chinese'}

Or use regex or lxml to parse the meta header:

<meta http\-equiv\="Content-Type" content\="text/html; charset=gbk" />

[curl\_cffi](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.10.0/index.html)

=====================================================================

# Compatibility with requests — curl_cffi  documentation

Compatibility with requests[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.0/vs-requests.html#compatibility-with-requests "Link to this heading")

================================================================================================================================================

Although we try our best to mimic the requests API, some functionality is not easy to implement and left out. Here are a list of known incompatibilities:

*   files API are slightly different, but more error-proof.
    
*   retries are not supported yet, tracked in \[#24\]([https://github.com/lexiforest/curl\_cffi/issues/24](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl_cffi/issues/24)
    )
    
*   redirect history are not supported, tracked in \[#82\]([https://github.com/lexiforest/curl\_cffi/issues/82](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl_cffi/issues/82)
    )
    
*   empty-domains cookies may lost during redirects, tracked in \[#55\]([https://github.com/lexiforest/curl\_cffi/issues/55](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl_cffi/issues/55)
    )
    
*   response object can not be pickled.
    

Alternatively, you can also use curl-cffi as a requests adapter via [curl-adapter](https://github.com/el1s7/curl-adapter)
. In this way, you get the full functionality of requests.

[curl\_cffi](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.0/index.html)

=====================================================================

# FAQ — curl_cffi  documentation

FAQ[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.1/faq.html#faq "Link to this heading")

========================================================================================

Why does the JA3 fingerprints change for Chrome 110+ impersonation?[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.1/faq.html#why-does-the-ja3-fingerprints-change-for-chrome-110-impersonation "Link to this heading")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This is intended.

Chrome introduces `ClientHello` permutation in version 110, which means the order of extensions will be random, thus JA3 fingerprints will be random. So, when comparing JA3 fingerprints of `curl_cffi` and a browser, they may differ. However, this does not mean that TLS fingerprints will not be a problem, `ClientHello` extension order is just one factor of how servers can tell automated requests from browsers.

Roughly, this can be mitigated like:

ja3 \= md5(list(extensions), ...other arguments)
ja3n \= md5(set(extensions), ...other arguments)

See more from [this article](https://www.fastly.com/blog/a-first-look-at-chromes-tls-clienthello-permutation-in-the-wild)
 and [curl-impersonate notes](https://github.com/lwthiker/curl-impersonate/pull/148)
.

Can I bypass Cloudflare with this project? or any other specific site.[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.1/faq.html#can-i-bypass-cloudflare-with-this-project-or-any-other-specific-site "Link to this heading")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Short answer is: it depends.

TLS and http2 fingerprints are just one of the many factors Cloudflare considers. Other factors include but are not limited to: IP quality, request rate, JS fingerprints, etc.

There are different protection levels for website owners to choose. For the most basic ones, TLS fingerprints alone maybe enough, but for higher levels, you may need to find a better proxy IP provider and use browser automation tools like playwright.

If you are in a hurry or just want the professionals to take care of the hard parts, you can consider the commercial solutions from our sponsors:

*   [Yescaptcha](https://yescaptcha.com/i/stfnIO)
    , captcha resolver and proxy service for bypassing Cloudflare.
    
*   [ScrapeNinja](https://scrapeninja.net/?utm_source=github&utm_medium=banner&utm_campaign=cffi)
    , Managed web scraping API.
    

For details, see the Sponsor section on front page.

I’m getting certs errors[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.1/faq.html#i-m-getting-certs-errors "Link to this heading")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The simplest way is to turn off cert verification by `verify=False`:

r \= curl\_cffi.get("https://example.com", verify\=False)

ErrCode: 77, Reason: error setting certificate verify locations[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.1/faq.html#errcode-77-reason-error-setting-certificate-verify-locations "Link to this heading")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Install `curl_cffi` and its dependencies in a pure-ASCII path, i.e. no Chinese or any other characters out of the ASCII table in the path.

How to use with fiddler/charles to intercept content[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.1/faq.html#how-to-use-with-fiddler-charles-to-intercept-content "Link to this heading")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Fiddler and Charles uses man-in-the-middle self-signed certs to intercept TLS traffic, to use with them, simply set `verify=False`.

ErrCode: 92, Reason: ‘HTTP/2 stream 0 was not closed cleanly: PROTOCOL\_ERROR (err 1)’[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.1/faq.html#errcode-92-reason-http-2-stream-0-was-not-closed-cleanly-protocol-error-err-1 "Link to this heading")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This error(http/2 stream 0) has been reported many times ever since curl\_cffi was published, but I still can not find a reproducible way to trigger it. Given that the majority users are behind proxies, the situation is even more difficult to deal with.

I’m even not sure it’s a bug introduced in libcurl, curl-impersonate or curl\_cffi, or it’s just a server error. Depending on your context, here are some general suggestions for you:

*   First, try removing the `Content-Length` header from you request.
    
*   Try to see if this error was caused by proxies, if so, use better proxies.
    
*   If it stops working after a while, maybe you’re just being blocked by, such as, Akamai.
    
*   Force http/1.1 mode. Some websites’ h2 implementation is simply broken.
    
*   See if the url works in your real browser.
    
*   Find a stable way to reproduce it, so we can finally fix, or at least bypass it.
    

To force curl to use http 1.1 only.

import curl\_cffi

r \= curl\_cffi.get("https://postman-echo.com", http\_version\=curl\_cffi.CurlHttpVersion.V1\_1)

Related issues:

*   [#19](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl_cffi/issues/19)
    ,
    
*   [#42](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl_cffi/issues/42)
    ,
    
*   [#79](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl_cffi/issues/79)
    ,

Packaging with PyInstaller[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.1/faq.html#packaging-with-pyinstaller "Link to this heading")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

If you encountered any issue with PyInstaller, here are a list of options provided by the community:

Add the `--hidden-import` option.

pyinstaller \-F .\\example.py \--hidden\-import\=\_cffi\_backend \--collect\-all curl\_cffi

Add other paths:

pyinstaller \--noconfirm \--onefile \--console \\
    \--paths "C:/Users/Administrator/AppData/Local/Programs/Python/Python39" \\
    \--add\-data "C:/Users/Administrator/AppData/Local/Programs/Python/Python39/Lib/site-packages/curl\_cffi.libs/libcurl-cbb416caa1dd01638554eab3f38d682d.dll;." \\
    \--collect\-data "curl\_cffi" \\
    "C:/Users/Administrator/Desktop/test\_script.py"

See also:

*   [#5](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl_cffi/issues/5)
    
*   [#48](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl_cffi/issues/48)
    

How to set proxy?[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.1/faq.html#how-to-set-proxy "Link to this heading")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You can use the `proxy` parameter:

import curl\_cffi

curl\_cffi.get(url, proxy\="http://user:pass@example.com:3128")

You can also use the `http_proxy`, `https_proxy`, and `ws_proxy`, `wss_proxy` environment variables, respectively.

For explanation of differences between `http_proxy` and `https_proxy`, please see [#6](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl_cffi/issues/6)
.

How to change the order of headers?[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.1/faq.html#how-to-change-the-order-of-headers "Link to this heading")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

By default, setting `impersonate` parameter will bring the corresponding headers. If you want to change the order or use your own headers, you need to turn off that and bring your own headers.

requests.get(url, impersonate\="chrome", default\_headers\=False, headers\=...)

How to deal with encoding/decoding errors?

Use `chardet` or `cchardet`

\>>> import curl\_cffi
\>>> r \= curl\_cffi.get("https://example.com/messy\_codec.html")
\>>> import chardet
\>>> chardet.detect(r.content)
{'encoding': 'GB2312', 'confidence': 0.99, 'language': 'Chinese'}

Or use regex or lxml to parse the meta header:

<meta http\-equiv\="Content-Type" content\="text/html; charset=gbk" />

[curl\_cffi](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.1/index.html)

# FAQ — curl_cffi  documentation

FAQ[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/faq.html#faq "Link to this heading")

=======================================================================================

Why does the JA3 fingerprints change for Chrome 110+ impersonation?[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/faq.html#why-does-the-ja3-fingerprints-change-for-chrome-110-impersonation "Link to this heading")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This is intended.

Chrome introduces `ClientHello` permutation in version 110, which means the order of extensions will be random, thus JA3 fingerprints will be random. So, when comparing JA3 fingerprints of `curl_cffi` and a browser, they may differ. However, this does not mean that TLS fingerprints will not be a problem, `ClientHello` extension order is just one factor of how servers can tell automated requests from browsers.

Roughly, this can be mitigated like:

ja3 \= md5(list(extensions), ...other arguments)
ja3n \= md5(set(extensions), ...other arguments)

See more from [this article](https://www.fastly.com/blog/a-first-look-at-chromes-tls-clienthello-permutation-in-the-wild)
 and [curl-impersonate notes](https://github.com/lwthiker/curl-impersonate/pull/148)
.

Can I bypass Cloudflare with this project? or any other specific site.[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/faq.html#can-i-bypass-cloudflare-with-this-project-or-any-other-specific-site "Link to this heading")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Short answer is: it depends.

TLS and http2 fingerprints are just one of the many factors Cloudflare considers. Other factors include but are not limited to: IP quality, request rate, JS fingerprints, etc.

There are different protection levels for website owners to choose. For the most basic ones, TLS fingerprints alone maybe enough, but for higher levels, you may need to find a better proxy IP provider and use browser automation tools like playwright.

If you are in a hurry or just want the professionals to take care of the hard parts, you can consider the commercial solutions from our sponsors:

*   [Scrapfly](https://scrapfly.io/?utm_source=github&utm_medium=sponsoring&utm_campaign=curl_cffi)
    , Cloud-based scraping platform.
    
*   [Yescaptcha](https://yescaptcha.com/i/stfnIO)
    , captcha resolver and proxy service for bypassing Cloudflare.
    
*   [ScrapeNinja](https://scrapeninja.net/?utm_source=github&utm_medium=banner&utm_campaign=cffi)
    , Managed web scraping API.
    

For details, see the Sponsor section on front page.

I’m getting certs errors[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/faq.html#i-m-getting-certs-errors "Link to this heading")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The simplest way is to turn off cert verification by `verify=False`:

r \= requests.get("https://example.com", verify\=False)

ErrCode: 77, Reason: error setting certificate verify locations[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/faq.html#errcode-77-reason-error-setting-certificate-verify-locations "Link to this heading")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Install `curl_cffi` and its dependencies in a pure-ASCII path, i.e. no Chinese or any other characters out of the ASCII table in the path.

How to use with fiddler/charles to intercept content[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/faq.html#how-to-use-with-fiddler-charles-to-intercept-content "Link to this heading")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Fiddler and Charles uses man-in-the-middle self-signed certs to intercept TLS traffic, to use with them, simply set `verify=False`.

ErrCode: 92, Reason: ‘HTTP/2 stream 0 was not closed cleanly: PROTOCOL\_ERROR (err 1)’[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/faq.html#errcode-92-reason-http-2-stream-0-was-not-closed-cleanly-protocol-error-err-1 "Link to this heading")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This error(http/2 stream 0) has been reported many times ever since curl\_cffi was published, but I still can not find a reproducible way to trigger it. Given that the majority users are behind proxies, the situation is even more difficult to deal with.

I’m even not sure it’s a bug introduced in libcurl, curl-impersonate or curl\_cffi, or it’s just a server error. Depending on your context, here are some general suggestions for you:

*   First, try removing the `Content-Length` header from you request.
    
*   Try to see if this error was caused by proxies, if so, use better proxies.
    
*   If it stops working after a while, maybe you’re just being blocked by, such as, Akamai.
    
*   Force http/1.1 mode. Some websites’ h2 implementation is simply broken.
    
*   See if the url works in your real browser.
    
*   Find a stable way to reproduce it, so we can finally fix, or at least bypass it.
    

To force curl to use http 1.1 only.

from curl\_cffi import requests, CurlHttpVersion

r \= requests.get("https://postman-echo.com", http\_version\=CurlHttpVersion.V1\_1)

Related issues:

*   [#19](https://github.com/yifeikong/curl_cffi/issues/19)
    ,
    
*   [#42](https://github.com/yifeikong/curl_cffi/issues/42)
    ,
    
*   [#79](https://github.com/yifeikong/curl_cffi/issues/79)
    ,
    
*   [#165](https://github.com/yifeikong/curl_cffi/issues/165)
    ,
    

Packaging with PyInstaller[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/faq.html#packaging-with-pyinstaller "Link to this heading")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

If you encountered any issue with PyInstaller, here are a list of options provided by the community:

Add the `--hidden-import` option.

pyinstaller \-F .\\example.py \--hidden\-import\=\_cffi\_backend \--collect\-all curl\_cffi

Add other paths:

pyinstaller \--noconfirm \--onefile \--console \\
    \--paths "C:/Users/Administrator/AppData/Local/Programs/Python/Python39" \\
    \--add\-data "C:/Users/Administrator/AppData/Local/Programs/Python/Python39/Lib/site-packages/curl\_cffi.libs/libcurl-cbb416caa1dd01638554eab3f38d682d.dll;." \\
    \--collect\-data "curl\_cffi" \\
    "C:/Users/Administrator/Desktop/test\_script.py"

See also:

*   [#5](https://github.com/yifeikong/curl_cffi/issues/5)
    
*   [#48](https://github.com/yifeikong/curl_cffi/issues/48)
    

How to set proxy?[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/faq.html#how-to-set-proxy "Link to this heading")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You can use the `proxy` parameter:

from curl\_cffi import requests

requests.get(url, proxy\="http://user:pass@example.com:3128")

You can also use the `http_proxy`, `https_proxy`, and `ws_proxy`, `wss_proxy` environment variables, respectively.

For explanation of differences between `http_proxy` and `https_proxy`, please see [#6](https://github.com/yifeikong/curl_cffi/issues/6)
.

How to change the order of headers?[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/faq.html#how-to-change-the-order-of-headers "Link to this heading")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

By default, setting `impersonate` parameter will bring the corresponding headers. If you want to change the order or use your own headers, you need to turn off that and bring your own headers.

requests.get(url, impersonate\="chrome", default\_headers\=False, headers\=...)

How to deal with encoding/decoding errors?

Use `chardet` or `cchardet`

\>>> from curl\_cffi import requests
\>>> r \= requests.get("https://example.com/messy\_codec.html")
\>>> import chardet
\>>> chardet.detect(r.content)
{'encoding': 'GB2312', 'confidence': 0.99, 'language': 'Chinese'}

Or use regex or lxml to parse the meta header:

<meta http\-equiv\="Content-Type" content\="text/html; charset=gbk" />

Compatibility with requests[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.12.0/vs-requests.html#compatibility-with-requests "Link to this heading")

================================================================================================================================================

Although we try our best to mimic the requests API, some functionality is not easy to implement and left out. Here are a list of known incompatibilities:

*   files API are slightly different, but more error-proof.
    
*   retries are not supported yet, tracked in \[#24\]([https://github.com/lexiforest/curl\_cffi/issues/24](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl_cffi/issues/24)
    )
    
*   redirect history are not supported, tracked in \[#82\]([https://github.com/lexiforest/curl\_cffi/issues/82](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl_cffi/issues/82)
    )
    
*   empty-domains cookies may lost during redirects, tracked in \[#55\]([https://github.com/lexiforest/curl\_cffi/issues/55](https://github.com/lexiforest/curl_cffi/issues/55)
    )
    
*   response object can not be pickled.
    
*   The `requests` proxies dict is supported, but we prefer `proxy=...`, unless you really use different proxies for http and https.
    
*   You can use use transports/adapters, instead, you can use `curl_cffi` as adapter for `reuqests`.
    

Transports and Adapters[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.12.0/vs-requests.html#transports-and-adapters "Link to this heading")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

`curl_cffi` is deeply coupled with `libcurl-impersonate`. Unlike `requests` or `httpx`, There is no way to use a different networking library or mount different adapters.

Alternatively, you can use `curl-cffi` as a requests adapter via [curl-adapter](https://github.com/el1s7/curl-adapter)
. In this way, you get the full functionality of requests.

# API References — curl_cffi  documentation

API References[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#api-references "Link to this heading")

=============================================================================================================

curl low levl APIs[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curl-low-levl-apis "Link to this heading")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Curl[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curl "Link to this heading")

_class_ curl\_cffi.Curl(_cacert: str \= ''_, _debug: bool \= False_, _handle\=None_)[\[source\]](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/_modules/curl_cffi/curl.html#Curl)
[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curl_cffi.Curl "Link to this definition")

Wrapper for `curl_easy_*` functions of libcurl.

\_\_init\_\_(_cacert: str \= ''_, _debug: bool \= False_, _handle\=None_) → None[\[source\]](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/_modules/curl_cffi/curl.html#Curl.__init__)
[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curl_cffi.Curl.__init__ "Link to this definition")

Parameters:

*   **cacert** – CA cert path to use, by default, curl\_cffi uses certs from `certifi`.
    
*   **debug** – whether to show curl debug messages.
    
*   **handle** – a curl handle instance from `curl_easy_init`.
    

debug() → None[\[source\]](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/_modules/curl_cffi/curl.html#Curl.debug)
[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curl_cffi.Curl.debug "Link to this definition")

Set debug to True

setopt(_option: [CurlOpt](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curl_cffi.CurlOpt "curl_cffi.const.CurlOpt")
_, _value: Any_) → int[\[source\]](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/_modules/curl_cffi/curl.html#Curl.setopt)
[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curl_cffi.Curl.setopt "Link to this definition")

Wrapper for `curl_easy_setopt`.

Parameters:

*   **option** – option to set, using constants from CurlOpt enum
    
*   **value** – value to set, strings will be handled automatically
    

Returns:

0 if no error, see `CurlECode`.

getinfo(_option: [CurlInfo](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curl_cffi.CurlInfo "curl_cffi.const.CurlInfo")
_) → bytes | int | float | List[\[source\]](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/_modules/curl_cffi/curl.html#Curl.getinfo)
[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curl_cffi.Curl.getinfo "Link to this definition")

Wrapper for `curl_easy_getinfo`. Gets information in response after curl perform.

Parameters:

**option** – option to get info of, using constants from `CurlInfo` enum

Returns:

value retrieved from last perform.

version() → bytes[\[source\]](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/_modules/curl_cffi/curl.html#Curl.version)
[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curl_cffi.Curl.version "Link to this definition")

Get the underlying libcurl version.

impersonate(_target: str_, _default\_headers: bool \= True_) → int[\[source\]](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/_modules/curl_cffi/curl.html#Curl.impersonate)
[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curl_cffi.Curl.impersonate "Link to this definition")

Set the browser type to impersonate.

Parameters:

*   **target** – browser to impersonate.
    
*   **default\_headers** – whether to add default headers, like User-Agent.
    

Returns:

0 if no error.

perform(_clear\_headers: bool \= True_) → None[\[source\]](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/_modules/curl_cffi/curl.html#Curl.perform)
[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curl_cffi.Curl.perform "Link to this definition")

Wrapper for `curl_easy_perform`, performs a curl request.

Parameters:

**clear\_headers** – clear header slist used in this perform

Raises:

**CurlError** – if the perform was not successful.

duphandle() → [Curl](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curl_cffi.Curl "curl_cffi.curl.Curl")
[\[source\]](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/_modules/curl_cffi/curl.html#Curl.duphandle)
[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curl_cffi.Curl.duphandle "Link to this definition")

Wrapper for `curl_easy_duphandle`.

This is not a full copy of entire curl object in python. For example, headers handle is not copied, you have to set them again.

reset() → None[\[source\]](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/_modules/curl_cffi/curl.html#Curl.reset)
[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curl_cffi.Curl.reset "Link to this definition")

Reset all curl options, wrapper for `curl_easy_reset`.

parse\_cookie\_headers(_headers: List\[bytes\]_) → SimpleCookie[\[source\]](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/_modules/curl_cffi/curl.html#Curl.parse_cookie_headers)
[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curl_cffi.Curl.parse_cookie_headers "Link to this definition")

Extract `cookies.SimpleCookie` from header lines.

Parameters:

**headers** – list of headers in bytes.

Returns:

A parsed cookies.SimpleCookie instance.

_static_ get\_reason\_phrase(_status\_line: bytes_) → bytes[\[source\]](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/_modules/curl_cffi/curl.html#Curl.get_reason_phrase)
[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curl_cffi.Curl.get_reason_phrase "Link to this definition")

Extract reason phrase, like `OK`, `Not Found` from response status line.

_static_ parse\_status\_line(_status\_line: bytes_) → Tuple\[[CurlHttpVersion](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curl_cffi.CurlHttpVersion "curl_cffi.const.CurlHttpVersion")\
, int, bytes\][\[source\]](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/_modules/curl_cffi/curl.html#Curl.parse_status_line)
[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curl_cffi.Curl.parse_status_line "Link to this definition")

Parse status line.

Returns:

http\_version, status\_code, and reason phrase

close() → None[\[source\]](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/_modules/curl_cffi/curl.html#Curl.close)
[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curl_cffi.Curl.close "Link to this definition")

Close and cleanup curl handle, wrapper for `curl_easy_cleanup`.

ws\_recv(_n: int \= 1024_) → Tuple\[bytes, Any\][\[source\]](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/_modules/curl_cffi/curl.html#Curl.ws_recv)
[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curl_cffi.Curl.ws_recv "Link to this definition")

Receive a frame from a websocket connection.

Parameters:

**n** – maximum data to receive.

Returns:

a tuple of frame content and curl frame meta struct.

Raises:

**CurlError** – if failed.

ws\_send(_payload: bytes_, _flags: [CurlWsFlag](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curl_cffi.CurlWsFlag "curl_cffi.const.CurlWsFlag")
 \= CurlWsFlag.BINARY_) → int[\[source\]](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/_modules/curl_cffi/curl.html#Curl.ws_send)
[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curl_cffi.Curl.ws_send "Link to this definition")

Send data to a websocket connection.

Parameters:

*   **payload** – content to send.
    
*   **flags** – websocket flag to set for the frame, default: binary.
    

Returns:

0 if no error.

Raises:

**CurlError** – if failed.

ws\_close() → None[\[source\]](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/_modules/curl_cffi/curl.html#Curl.ws_close)
[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curl_cffi.Curl.ws_close "Link to this definition")

Send the close frame.

### AsyncCurl[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#asynccurl "Link to this heading")

_class_ curl\_cffi.AsyncCurl(_cacert: str \= ''_, _loop\=None_)[\[source\]](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/_modules/curl_cffi/aio.html#AsyncCurl)
[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curl_cffi.AsyncCurl "Link to this definition")

Wrapper around curl\_multi handle to provide asyncio support. It uses the libcurl socket\_action APIs.

\_\_init\_\_(_cacert: str \= ''_, _loop\=None_)[\[source\]](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/_modules/curl_cffi/aio.html#AsyncCurl.__init__)
[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curl_cffi.AsyncCurl.__init__ "Link to this definition")

Parameters:

*   **cacert** – CA cert path to use, by default, curl\_cffi uses certs from `certifi`.
    
*   **loop** – EventLoop to use.
    

add\_handle(_curl: [Curl](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curl_cffi.Curl "curl_cffi.curl.Curl")
_)[\[source\]](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/_modules/curl_cffi/aio.html#AsyncCurl.add_handle)
[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curl_cffi.AsyncCurl.add_handle "Link to this definition")

Add a curl handle to be managed by curl\_multi. This is the equivalent of perform in the async world.

remove\_handle(_curl: [Curl](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curl_cffi.Curl "curl_cffi.curl.Curl")
_)[\[source\]](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/_modules/curl_cffi/aio.html#AsyncCurl.remove_handle)
[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curl_cffi.AsyncCurl.remove_handle "Link to this definition")

Cancel a future for given curl handle.

set\_result(_curl: [Curl](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curl_cffi.Curl "curl_cffi.curl.Curl")
_)[\[source\]](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/_modules/curl_cffi/aio.html#AsyncCurl.set_result)
[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curl_cffi.AsyncCurl.set_result "Link to this definition")

Mark a future as done for given curl handle.

set\_exception(_curl: [Curl](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curl_cffi.Curl "curl_cffi.curl.Curl")
_, _exception_)[\[source\]](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/_modules/curl_cffi/aio.html#AsyncCurl.set_exception)
[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curl_cffi.AsyncCurl.set_exception "Link to this definition")

Raise exception of a future for given curl handle.

setopt(_option_, _value_)[\[source\]](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/_modules/curl_cffi/aio.html#AsyncCurl.setopt)
[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curl_cffi.AsyncCurl.setopt "Link to this definition")

Wrapper around curl\_multi\_setopt.

socket\_action(_sockfd: int_, _ev\_bitmask: int_) → int[\[source\]](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/_modules/curl_cffi/aio.html#AsyncCurl.socket_action)
[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curl_cffi.AsyncCurl.socket_action "Link to this definition")

Call libcurl socket\_action function

process\_data(_sockfd: int_, _ev\_bitmask: int_)[\[source\]](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/_modules/curl_cffi/aio.html#AsyncCurl.process_data)
[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curl_cffi.AsyncCurl.process_data "Link to this definition")

Call curl\_multi\_info\_read to read data for given socket.

_async_ close()[\[source\]](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/_modules/curl_cffi/aio.html#AsyncCurl.close)
[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curl_cffi.AsyncCurl.close "Link to this definition")

Close and cleanup running timers, readers, writers and handles.

### CurlMime[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curlmime "Link to this heading")

_class_ curl\_cffi.CurlMime(_curl: [Curl](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curl_cffi.Curl "curl_cffi.curl.Curl")
 | None \= None_)[\[source\]](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/_modules/curl_cffi/curl.html#CurlMime)
[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curl_cffi.CurlMime "Link to this definition")

Wrapper for the `curl_mime_` API.

\_\_init\_\_(_curl: [Curl](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curl_cffi.Curl "curl_cffi.curl.Curl")
 | None \= None_)[\[source\]](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/_modules/curl_cffi/curl.html#CurlMime.__init__)
[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curl_cffi.CurlMime.__init__ "Link to this definition")

Parameters:

**curl** – Curl instance to use.

addpart(_name: str_, _\*_, _content\_type: str | None \= None_, _filename: str | None \= None_, _local\_path: str | bytes | Path | None \= None_, _data: bytes | None \= None_) → None[\[source\]](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/_modules/curl_cffi/curl.html#CurlMime.addpart)
[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curl_cffi.CurlMime.addpart "Link to this definition")

Add a mime part for a mutlipart html form.

Note: You can only use either local\_path or data, not both.

Parameters:

*   **name** – name of the field.
    
*   **content\_type** – content\_type for the field. for example: `image/png`.
    
*   **filename** – filename for the server.
    
*   **local\_path** – file to upload on local disk.
    
*   **data** – file content to upload.
    

_classmethod_ from\_list(_files: List\[dict\]_)[\[source\]](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/_modules/curl_cffi/curl.html#CurlMime.from_list)
[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curl_cffi.CurlMime.from_list "Link to this definition")

Create a multipart instance from a list of dict, for keys, see `addpart`

attach(_curl: [Curl](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curl_cffi.Curl "curl_cffi.curl.Curl")
 | None \= None_) → None[\[source\]](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/_modules/curl_cffi/curl.html#CurlMime.attach)
[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curl_cffi.CurlMime.attach "Link to this definition")

Attach the mime instance to a curl instance.

close() → None[\[source\]](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/_modules/curl_cffi/curl.html#CurlMime.close)
[¶](https://curl-cffi.readthedocs.io/en/v0.7.1/api.html#curl_cffi.CurlMime.close "Link to this definition")

Close the mime instance and underlying files. This method must be called after `perform` or `request`.
